Sencha Touch.history: Sencha Touch was a mobile web JavaScript framework released around 2010 by Sencha Inc. Sencha Inc. was later acquired by Idera in 2017. Sencha Touch itself has been deprecated in favor of Ext JS and is no longer actively developed.
Sencha Touch.summary_abuse: Sencha Touch has no notable abuse signal. It is a deprecated mobile web JavaScript framework with no consumer-facing or content-hosting surface.

Send.history: Send is a WordPress-native marketing automation and campaign management platform available at send2.co. It is a niche tool within the WordPress ecosystem with no notable founding details or ownership events publicly documented.
Send.summary_abuse: Send is a marketing automation platform for WordPress. As an email/campaign tool, it carries the standard marketing-platform spam risk if used for bulk unsolicited outreach, but there are no documented specific abuse incidents.

Sendgrid.history: SendGrid was founded in 2009 by Isaac Saldana, Jose Lopez, and Tim Jenkins as an email delivery service. It went public in 2017 (NYSE: SEND) and was acquired by Twilio in 2019 for approximately $3 billion.
Sendgrid.summary_abuse: SendGrid is occasionally used for spam and phishing due to its high deliverability and large user base, as is common for major email delivery platforms. Legitimate use overwhelmingly dominates. Twilio/SendGrid maintains an active abuse team, publishes AUP enforcement reports, and responds to abuse complaints. Its dominant market position means it is both a target for abusers and a platform with the resources to combat it.

SendHeap.history: SendHeap is a multi-channel customer notification platform for ecommerce businesses, covering order updates, campaign alerts, and price change communications. No founding details or notable events are publicly documented.
SendHeap.summary_abuse: SendHeap is a transactional notification and marketing messaging platform for ecommerce. As a multi-channel communication tool, it carries some risk of misuse for spam campaigns, but no specific documented abuse incidents are known.

Sendinblue.history: Sendinblue was founded in 2012 in Paris, France as a transactional email provider and later expanded into a full multi-channel marketing platform. In 2023 the company rebranded to Brevo, positioning itself as a broader CRM and marketing suite.
Sendinblue.summary_abuse: Sendinblue (now Brevo) has moderate abuse risk as a major email marketing platform with a free tier. Bulk email platforms are commonly used for spam and phishing campaigns. Sendinblue has documented AUP enforcement, anti-spam filtering, and GDPR-compliant processes. Its European base and focus on GDPR compliance somewhat differentiate it from less rigorous competitors.

Sendlane.history: Sendlane was founded in 2013 in San Diego as an email and SMS marketing automation platform focused on ecommerce merchants. It has raised venture funding and targets Shopify and BigCommerce merchants specifically.
Sendlane.summary_abuse: Sendlane is an email and SMS marketing platform primarily used by legitimate ecommerce businesses. Like all bulk email platforms it can theoretically be used for spam, but Sendlane requires opt-in list practices and monitors deliverability. No specific documented abuse incidents stand out beyond typical email-marketing platform risks.

Sendloop.history: Sendloop was founded in 2008 by Cem and Mert Hurturk as a custom email marketing and delivery platform serving 36,000+ customers across 100+ countries. No notable ownership changes have occurred since founding.
Sendloop.summary_abuse: Sendloop is an email marketing platform with bulk email delivery capabilities. Like other email marketing services, it carries a risk of being used for spam if anti-abuse controls are insufficient. No specific abuse incidents are publicly documented, and the platform serves a legitimate SMB customer base.

SendOwl.history: SendOwl (formerly known as Digital Delivery App) was founded around 2010 in the UK as a platform for selling digital products and subscriptions online, providing payment links and delivery automation. No significant ownership changes are documented.
SendOwl.summary_abuse: SendOwl is used primarily by legitimate creators selling digital downloads. It has no documented history of being a preferred platform for fraud, though like any digital-goods platform it could theoretically host illicit content. The vendor enforces an acceptable use policy.

Sendpad.history: Sendpad is an automated audience engagement and personalization platform for marketers. Its upstream website entry points to sendtex.com, suggesting possible overlap or rebranding with Sendtex. No founding details are publicly documented.
Sendpad.summary_abuse: Sendpad is a marketing personalization and audience engagement platform. As an email/messaging tool, it carries standard bulk-messaging spam risk, but no specific documented abuse incidents are known.

SendPulse.history: SendPulse was founded in 2015, initially in Ukraine, as an email marketing platform. It expanded to include SMS, web push, and chatbot channels and has grown to a mainstream multi-channel marketing tool.
SendPulse.summary_abuse: SendPulse has moderate abuse risk as a multi-channel marketing platform with email, SMS, and chatbot features. Bulk email and SMS platforms are frequently abused for spam and phishing. The platform has a free tier which can be used by spammers. SendPulse has AUP provisions but enforcement visibility is limited.

Sendtex.history: Sendtex is a GDPR-compliant email marketing platform using European servers, targeting businesses needing high-volume email sending (150,000+ per month). No founding year or notable ownership events are publicly documented.
Sendtex.summary_abuse: Sendtex is an email marketing platform with GDPR compliance focus. As a bulk email sending platform, it carries standard spam risk if abused, but GDPR compliance and European infrastructure suggest reasonable anti-abuse posture. No specific incidents documented.

SendX.history: SendX is an Indian email marketing SaaS founded around 2017, targeting small and mid-size businesses with campaign building, mailing list management, and marketing automation. No notable ownership changes are documented.
SendX.summary_abuse: SendX is a small email marketing platform with moderate abuse risk; like all bulk email services, it can be used by marketers sending unwanted commercial email, though it is primarily used by legitimate SMBs. Its paid-only model reduces casual spam abuse compared to freemium email platforms.

Senja.history: Senja launched in 2021 as a testimonial collection and social proof platform, targeting creators and SaaS businesses. It has grown to support integrations with 30+ platforms including Shopify and G2.
Senja.summary_abuse: Senja is a testimonials and social proof platform with no notable abuse signal. Its use case (collecting and displaying customer reviews) has minimal fraud surface area.

Sensai Metrics.history: Sensai Metrics is an ecommerce analytics SaaS that uses data science and machine learning to provide predictive insights for online stores. No founding year or notable events are publicly documented.
Sensai Metrics.summary_abuse: Sensai Metrics is an ecommerce analytics platform with no consumer-facing user-generated content surface. It has no notable abuse signal.

Sensor Tower.history: Sensor Tower was founded in 2013 by Oliver Yeh and Alex Malafeev as a mobile app store intelligence platform. The company raised $45M from Riverwood Capital and has acquired both AppMagic and data.ai (formerly App Annie) to consolidate the mobile analytics market.
Sensor Tower.summary_abuse: Sensor Tower has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise-grade mobile analytics intelligence platform with no consumer-facing or content-hosting features.

Sensors Data.history: Sensors Data (神策数据) is a Chinese enterprise analytics platform founded in 2015 by former Baidu engineers, providing user behaviour tracking and customer data analytics primarily for Chinese enterprises; it has grown to serve major Chinese internet companies and corporations. No notable ownership changes are documented.
Sensors Data.summary_abuse: Sensors Data is a Chinese enterprise analytics platform with no notable abuse signal in terms of fraud or phishing; it is a B2B analytics backend for enterprise clients. However, its Chinese jurisdiction and data practices mean GDPR-scope users should evaluate data residency carefully.

Sentara.history: Sentara Health traces its roots to 1888 when the Norfolk Women's Christian Association founded a hospital in Norfolk, Virginia. The modern corporate entity was formed in 1972 when Norfolk General Hospital and Leigh Memorial Hospital merged as Medical Center Hospitals, later rebranded Sentara. It grew into one of the top 20 largest not-for-profit integrated health systems in the US, with 34,000 employees and 12 hospitals across Virginia and North Carolina.
Sentara.summary_abuse: Sentara is a large nonprofit healthcare provider with no notable abuse signal as a technology. Its web platform serves patients and staff in a regulated healthcare context; it is not a consumer-facing tool that fraudsters would exploit for phishing kits or scam infrastructure.

Sentifi.history: Sentifi was founded in 2012 in Zurich, Switzerland, as a fintech platform using crowd-sourced financial intelligence to provide market insights. The company targets institutional investors and financial services firms with its analytics platform.
Sentifi.summary_abuse: Sentifi is a B2B financial analytics platform with no notable abuse signal. It serves institutional financial market participants and has no consumer-facing surface that would attract phishing or fraud activity.

Sentry.history: Sentry was created in 2008 as an open-source error-tracking tool by David Cramer at Disqus. It was spun out as a standalone company (Functional Software, Inc.) in 2012 with VC backing. In 2019 it controversially relicensed from BSD to the Business Source License (BSL), restricting cloud competitors from hosting it commercially. It remains one of the most widely used error monitoring platforms globally.
Sentry.summary_abuse: Sentry is an error-monitoring and application-performance tool used by development teams. It has no meaningful abuse signal as a platform for fraud or scams. Its main data-handling concern is that it can inadvertently capture PII in error payloads (stack traces, request bodies), but this is a data-hygiene issue, not a fraud-platform risk.

SEO Manager.history: SEO Manager is a Shopify app developed by Venntov, a small development studio. It provides automated SEO optimization tools for Shopify store owners and has been available in the Shopify App Store for several years.
SEO Manager.summary_abuse: SEO Manager is a Shopify app with no notable abuse signal. It is a utility tool for legitimate ecommerce merchants with no consumer-facing hosting surface.

SEO Samba.history: SEO Samba is a marketing automation platform designed for franchise and multi-location businesses. It was founded to address the need for centralized SEO and local marketing management at scale for franchise networks.
SEO Samba.summary_abuse: SEO Samba is a franchise SEO automation platform with no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B tool for legitimate multi-location businesses and does not provide consumer-facing hosting that could be exploited.

SEOAnt.history: SEOAnt is a Shopify-focused SEO app launched around 2019, targeting ecommerce store owners seeking search ranking improvements. It is operated by a small team, likely based in China, and distributed through the Shopify App Store.
SEOAnt.summary_abuse: SEOAnt is a Shopify SEO app with minimal abuse signal. It runs within the Shopify ecosystem and does not provide consumer-facing hosting. Its primary risk is data privacy given uncertain data handling policies.

SEOmatic.history: SEOmatic is a SEO plugin for Craft CMS developed by nystudio107 (Andrew Welch). It is a Craft CMS community staple providing structured SEO metadata, JSON-LD, and sitemap generation as a one-time purchase plugin.
SEOmatic.summary_abuse: SEOmatic is a Craft CMS plugin with no notable abuse signal. It is a server-side SEO utility with no public-facing hosting surface for fraudulent use.

SEOmator.history: SEOmator is an AI-powered SEO audit and analysis platform. It positions itself as a white-label SEO tool for agencies, offering site audits, keyword tracking, and competitor analysis.
SEOmator.summary_abuse: SEOmator is an SEO analysis SaaS with no notable abuse signal. It is used by agencies for site auditing and does not provide consumer-facing hosting.

Seon.history: SEON was founded in January 2017 in Budapest, Hungary by Tamas Kadar and Bence Jendruszak, who developed the platform after encountering fraud challenges while running a cryptocurrency exchange. The company raised a Series A led by Creandum in 2021, a Series B led by IVP in 2022, and an $80 million Series C led by Sixth Street Partners in September 2025. It expanded internationally with offices in Austin TX, London, and Jakarta, growing to over 300 employees serving 5,000+ businesses.
Seon.summary_abuse: SEON is a fraud prevention and AML compliance platform; its purpose is to detect and block fraud rather than enable it. The platform processes fraud signals for client businesses and has no consumer-facing surface that would be exploited by bad actors. It holds SOC 2 Type 2 and ISO 27001 certifications and is GDPR compliant, indicating mature security governance. No abuse incidents involving SEON itself have been documented.

SEOPress.history: SEOPress is a WordPress SEO plugin created by Benjamin Denis in France, first released around 2017. It competes with Yoast SEO and Rank Math as a privacy-friendly alternative with GDPR-conscious design.
SEOPress.summary_abuse: SEOPress is a WordPress SEO plugin with no notable abuse signal. It is a server-side plugin that modifies site metadata and sitemaps with no consumer-facing hosting surface for abuse.

Seosphera.history: Seosphera is a web development platform for building and deploying websites and web applications. No founding details or notable ownership events are publicly documented.
Seosphera.summary_abuse: Seosphera is a web development and site-building platform with no notable abuse signal and no documented fraudulent use cases.

SeoToaster CMS.history: SeoToaster CMS is an open-source PHP CMS and ecommerce platform developed by Seosamba, a US-based web software company. It is distributed under GPLv3 and positions itself as an SEO-focused alternative to WordPress with an integrated shopping cart. The project is actively maintained with regular releases, reaching version 3.12 in early 2026.
SeoToaster CMS.summary_abuse: SeoToaster CMS is a self-hosted open-source CMS with no notable abuse signal. As a PHP-based platform requiring server deployment, it lacks the consumer-facing shared hosting environment that attracts phishing kit operators. No significant abuse incidents have been documented.

SEP Platform.history: SEP Platform is an ecommerce system with limited public information available. It is a small, regional ecommerce product.
SEP Platform.summary_abuse: SEP Platform is an ecommerce SaaS with no notable abuse signal. It is a merchant-facing platform with no documented history of exploitation.

SeQura.history: SeQura was founded in 2013 in Barcelona as a FinTech company providing buy-now-pay-later and flexible payment solutions for online merchants, with a focus on Southern Europe and Latin America. It has raised significant VC funding and expanded across Spain, Italy, and several Latin American markets.
SeQura.summary_abuse: SeQura is a regulated FinTech BNPL provider operating in Southern Europe and Latin America. As a payment infrastructure provider it has no notable abuse signal; fraud risk is mitigated by its lending-model KYC and credit checks on end consumers. No documented phishing or scam infrastructure use.

Seravo.history: Founded in 2011 in Tampere, Finland, initially focusing on Linux server upkeep before launching its Premium WordPress Hosting and Upkeep service in 2014. Remained an independent privately held company with no notable acquisitions. Actively contributes to open source and the WordPress community through event sponsorship, plugin development, and code contributions.
Seravo.summary_abuse: Seravo is a premium managed WordPress hosting provider with a robust security posture. The platform includes automatic daily backups, 24/7 monitoring, free malware cleanup via its security guarantee, and automated security updates. Starting at 25 EUR/month with no free tier and no anonymous signup, it is not a preferred platform for abuse. Located in Finland with strong GDPR privacy protections and a published security FAQ, the vendor demonstrates proactive anti-abuse measures. No notable public incidents of widespread abuse were identified.

Serchen.history: Serchen was established in 1997 as one of the first software marketplaces on the web, helping buyers find business software. It has operated continuously for nearly three decades, growing to list over 56,000 software apps and over a million reviews. The platform serves both US and UK markets through separate domains. No notable ownership changes or acquisitions have occurred.
Serchen.summary_abuse: Serchen is a software marketplace and review platform that lists over 56,000 business software products. As a user-generated review platform, it carries theoretical risk of fake or incentivized reviews, but no documented patterns of large-scale abuse have been publicly reported. The platform operates with editorial oversight of reviews and listings. It does not offer anonymous content hosting, custom domains, or other features commonly exploited by abusers.

Serendipity.history: Serendipity was first released in 2002 as a PHP-powered weblog engine, developed by a community team with German roots. It offers a plugin and theme architecture and has maintained a small but loyal user base. The project continues with incremental updates, with version 2.6 as the current stable release. No notable ownership changes or forks have occurred.
Serendipity.summary_abuse: Serendipity is a self-hosted PHP blogging system with no consumer-facing accounts, free tier hosting, or content delivery features. As a backend CMS installed by blog owners on their own servers, it has no notable abuse signal or documented pattern of exploitation for phishing, scams, or malware.

Service Management Group.history: Founded in 1998 in Kansas City, Missouri as a customer experience measurement firm. Has since expanded into unified experience management covering brand, customer, and employee experience through the Ignite AI-native platform. No notable ownership changes or acquisitions.
Service Management Group.summary_abuse: SMG is an enterprise B2B experience management platform serving large brands. It requires enterprise contracts with formal procurement processes and offers no consumer-facing free tier or anonymous signup. Survey platforms occasionally host fraudulent surveys but SMG's enterprise-only model limits this vector. No notable public abuse incidents documented.

Service Provider Pro.history: Founded in 2014 by Chris Willow as Service Provider Pro, initially a self-hosted software for managing his own video production agency. Later transitioned to SaaS and rebranded as Wayfront. Bootstrapped, no outside investment, profitable from inception.
Service Provider Pro.summary_abuse: Service Provider Pro / Wayfront has minimal abuse signal as a client management and billing SaaS for digital marketing agencies. It requires paid subscription, offers no free tier with custom domains, does not support anonymous signup, and does not host public-facing user content. As a B2B platform for agency-client workflows, fraud and phishing use is low. No notable abuse incidents documented.

ServiceCore.history: ServiceCore is a Denver-based SaaS for portable toilet, septic, and dumpster service businesses. Founded around 2016, it provides job management, route optimization, inventory tracking, and billing. No notable ownership changes.
ServiceCore.summary_abuse: ServiceCore is a niche operational SaaS for sanitation and waste service businesses with no abuse signal. It manages jobs, routes, and billing for service operators. It has no free tier, anonymous signup, or consumer-facing content hosting.

ServiceM8.history: Founded in 2009 by Kim Ford and his son Ben Ford in Australia after Kim bought a locksmith business and found no suitable job management software. The first version launched as an iPhone and web app in 2010. ServiceM8 has won multiple awards including Xero Small Business App of the Year (2015, 2021, 2023) and was named in Deloitte's Tech Fast 50 in 2020. No acquisition or ownership changes since founding.
ServiceM8.summary_abuse: ServiceM8 is a B2B field service management platform for trade contractors with no documented abuse signal. It serves legitimate small businesses managing jobs, scheduling, and invoicing. The platform offers no consumer-facing content hosting, no URL shortening, no anonymous signup, and no mass-email features. Its free tier is limited to 30 jobs per month and requires a verified business account, making it unsuitable as a vector for fraud or phishing.

ServiceNow.history: Founded in 2003 by Fred Luddy as Glidesoft, renamed ServiceNow in 2006. Went public via NYSE IPO in June 2012. Under CEOs Frank Slootman, John Donahoe, and Bill McDermott (2019-present), grew to dominant enterprise IT service management platform with $13.28B revenue (2025) and ~29k employees. Acquired dozens of companies including Element AI (2020), Lightstep (2021), Moveworks (~$3B, 2025), and Armis (~$7.75B, 2026) expanding into AI, observability, and security.
ServiceNow.summary_abuse: ServiceNow has minimal abuse signal. It is an enterprise SaaS platform for IT service management and workflow automation with no consumer-facing surface. The primary documented abuse vector is misconfigured knowledge base instances exposing corporate data, as reported in 2024 affecting over 1,000 instances (BleepingComputer). ServiceNow maintains security compliance programs (SOC 2, ISO 27001) and a responsible disclosure policy, responding to reported vulnerabilities. No significant phishing, scam, or fraud infrastructure use is associated with the platform.

Session Rewind.history: Founded around 2020 as an affordable session recording and analytics platform. Session Rewind provides session playback, heatmaps, error tracking, and funnel analytics as an alternative to FullStory, LogRocket, and Hotjar. The platform offers a free tier with 3,000 sessions per month.
Session Rewind.summary_abuse: Session Rewind is a session recording and analytics SaaS that requires website integration and account registration. As a user behavior analytics tool, it collects visitor interaction data on client websites. Session recording tools carry inherent privacy considerations as they capture user browsing behavior, but Session Rewind operates as a B2B analytics service for legitimate businesses. No notable abuse incidents documented.

Sessionly.history: Founded in 2017 in Austria as a loyalty and referral program for Shopware and Shopify merchants. No notable ownership changes, acquisitions, or deprecation events since.
Sessionly.summary_abuse: Sessionly is a niche loyalty and referral SaaS for Shopify and Shopware merchants with no meaningful abuse signal. It operates as a backend integration tool embedded in e-commerce stores with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters typically target. No documented abuse incidents or security research reports were found.

SessionStack.history: SessionStack was originally developed as an AI-enhanced analytics platform for online retailers. The product has since pivoted and the domain sessionstack.com now redirects to PlaybookUX, a user experience research platform serving enterprise clients including Microsoft and Google.
SessionStack.summary_abuse: SessionStack's original platform was a backend analytics tool for online retailers with no consumer-facing surface, custom domain hosting, or anonymous signup. The current PlaybookUX site carries minimal abuse risk as a B2B research platform without consumer-facing hosting features. No documented abuse patterns are associated with either product.

Setmore.history: Founded in 2011, Setmore provides cloud-based appointment scheduling software headquartered in Raleigh, North Carolina. It operates as a subsidiary of AnywhereWorks alongside other business tools. The platform has grown steadily with no major ownership changes or deprecation events.
Setmore.summary_abuse: Setmore has minimal abuse signal. It is a mainstream SMB scheduling tool used primarily for appointment booking. The free tier offers custom booking page URLs and SMS/email reminders, features that could theoretically be misused, but no significant abuse patterns are documented. Setmore maintains a standard Acceptable Use Policy and provides 24/7 human support for abuse reporting. Legitimate business use overwhelmingly dominates.

Setrics.history: Setrics is a US-based omni-channel marketing analytics platform headquartered in Provo, Utah. The company was founded around 2014 and maintains three Utah offices. No notable ownership changes or deprecation events since founding.
Setrics.summary_abuse: Setrics is a marketing analytics and performance platform with no notable abuse signal. It does not offer public signup with free hosting of user-generated content, custom domains, or link-shortening features that fraudsters commonly exploit. The platform requires a sales-led onboarding process (request demo), which naturally limits anonymous abuse. No documented instances of Setrics being used for phishing, scams, or malware delivery were found.

Setrow.history: Founded circa 2013 in Istanbul as Teknolog Bilgi Teknolojileri ve Sistemleri A.S. Provides an integrated digital marketing platform with email, SMS, push notification, pop-up, banner, and recommendation engine services. Serves clients including Starbucks, Aselsan, and Pierre Cardin across Turkey, UAE, UK, Netherlands, and Poland. ISO certified with KVKK (Turkish data protection) compliance. No notable ownership changes.
Setrow.summary_abuse: Setrow is an omnichannel marketing automation SaaS offering email marketing, SMS marketing, push notifications, and WhatsApp marketing services. As a mass-communication platform, it carries inherent abuse potential through spam or unauthorized messaging. The company publishes an anti-spam policy, has KVKK (Turkish data protection law) compliance, and requires opt-in consent for messaging. Clients include legitimate enterprises such as Starbucks and Aselsan. No notable public incidents of abuse have been documented. The platform's terms prohibit spam and unauthorized messaging.

SEUR.history: SEUR is a Spanish express delivery company founded in 1942. It became part of DPDgroup, a subsidiary of La Poste (the French national postal service), making it part of the broader European parcel delivery network.
SEUR.summary_abuse: SEUR is a Spanish logistics and shipping carrier with no notable abuse signal. Its shipping tracking widget embeds are benign. Phishing emails occasionally impersonate SEUR delivery notifications but this is brand impersonation rather than platform abuse.

SevenRooms.history: Founded in 2011 in New York by Joel Montaniel, Allison Page, and Kinesh Patel as a CRM and operations platform for hospitality. Raised funding from Comcast Ventures, Amazon's Alexa Fund, and PSG. Acquired HeyPluto in January 2024 to expand AI messaging. Acquired by DoorDash for $1.2 billion in June 2025.
SevenRooms.summary_abuse: SevenRooms is a B2B hospitality CRM and operations platform with minimal abuse signal. It has no consumer-facing free tier, no custom-domain hosting, and no anonymous signup capability that fraudsters typically exploit. The platform handles sensitive guest PII and payment data, and its trust portal confirms SOC 2 Type 2, PCI DSS, and GDPR compliance. A responsible disclosure policy is available at security@sevenrooms.com, but no public bug bounty program was identified. No notable public incidents of abuse or fraud were found during research.

Sezzle.history: Founded 2016 in Minneapolis by Charlie Youakim and co-founders as an interest-free BNPL installment platform. Listed on the Australian Securities Exchange in July 2019 and later on Nasdaq (SEZL) as a public benefit corporation. Agreed to be acquired by Zip Co in February 2022 for A$491M but the deal was mutually terminated in July 2022. Hindenburg Research published a critical report in December 2024 alleging risky lending practices and insider stock sales. Filed an antitrust lawsuit against Shopify in June 2025.
Sezzle.summary_abuse: Sezzle has documented abuse signal primarily through payments processed for online pharmacies flagged as 'rogue' by the National Association of Boards of Pharmacy and LegitScript, which ship prescription drugs to US consumers without valid prescriptions. Hindenburg Research also reported concerns about high-risk lending to subprime borrowers with deteriorating credit quality. The platform has been accused of enrolling users into recurring subscriptions without clear consent. Sezzle operates under state lending licenses and publishes an Acceptable Use Policy, but has faced scrutiny over merchant oversight and chargeback disputes related to pharmacy transactions. Legitimate BNPL use overwhelmingly dominates, with partnerships at major retailers like Amazon, Target, and Walmart.

shadcn-svelte.history: shadcn-svelte is a community port of shadcn/ui adapted for Svelte, created in 2023. It provides the same copy-paste component philosophy for Svelte developers.
shadcn-svelte.summary_abuse: shadcn-svelte has no notable abuse signal; it is a client-side Svelte component library.

shadcn/ui.history: shadcn/ui was created by shadcn in 2023 as a reusable component collection built with Radix UI primitives and Tailwind CSS. Unlike traditional component libraries, it ships components as copy-paste code rather than an npm package. It rapidly became one of the most starred JavaScript projects on GitHub.
shadcn/ui.summary_abuse: shadcn/ui has no notable abuse signal; it is a client-side React component library with no server-side component.

Shaka Player.history: Created by the Google Chrome team in 2014 as an open-source JavaScript library for adaptive media playback using MSE and EME. Originally developed within Google, it later moved to the independent shaka-project organization. Maintained collaboratively by Google, Ateme, and Paramount. No major forks, ownership changes, or deprecation events since.
Shaka Player.summary_abuse: Shaka Player has no notable abuse signal; it is a client-side JavaScript library for adaptive video playback that runs in the browser and does not host content, support custom domains, or expose features commonly exploited by fraudsters. It has no user accounts, free tier, or URL-redirect capabilities.

Shanon.history: Founded August 2000 in Tokyo as Shanon Inc. Launched SHANON MARKETING PLATFORM in 2011. Listed on TSE Mothers (now TSE Growth) in January 2017. Acquired Vibbit Interactive (CMS business) and TAGGY's advertising business in 2021. Major shareholder is Innovation Inc.
Shanon.summary_abuse: Shanon is a Japanese enterprise marketing automation platform with no notable abuse signal. It serves primarily B2B clients through direct sales, offers no free tier with custom domains, and requires KYC for onboarding. The company holds ISO 27001 certification and maintains a published security policy. As a subscription-based SaaS operating mainly in Japan, it has minimal exposure to phishing, scam, or spam abuse.

Shapecss.history: Created in 2016 by Shahanur Sharif under MicroDreamIT as a modern flexbox-based CSS framework. No notable ownership changes, forks, or deprecation events since.
Shapecss.summary_abuse: Shapecss is a CSS framework with no abuse signal; it provides static stylesheets with no consumer-facing surface or hosting capability that fraudsters would target.

Shapo.history: Shapo was developed by Configo Ltd., an Israeli company, with the product launching around 2023 as a testimonial collection and display platform. It provides embeddable review widgets and integrates with major review sources including Google, Facebook, G2, and Trustpilot. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes.
Shapo.summary_abuse: Shapo is a testimonial management SaaS with minimal abuse signal. The free tier is limited to 10 testimonials with no anonymous signup or custom-domain content hosting. The platform collects and displays customer reviews, which offers limited utility for fraud or phishing operations. The vendor publishes a DPA and accepts abuse reports via email, though no formal bug bounty or responsible-disclosure program was identified.

Shareaholic.history: Founded in 2009 by Jay Meattle as a nights-and-weekends project, Shareaholic grew into a content amplification and monetization platform serving over 300,000 websites. The company is VC-backed by General Catalyst, Kepha Partners, NextView Ventures, and 500 Startups. Headquartered in Cambridge, Massachusetts, Shareaholic operates as an independent privately held company with no known acquisition or ownership changes.
Shareaholic.summary_abuse: Shareaholic is a mainstream content marketing platform used by over 300,000 legitimate websites. It offers a branded URL shortener and social sharing widgets that could be repurposed for abuse, but requires email registration (no anonymous signup) and URL shortening uses branded domains with pixel tracking, reducing anonymity. The platform collects browsing data for interest-based advertising, which is typical ad-tech behavior rather than abuse. No notable public incidents of widespread abuse tied to Shareaholic have been documented. The primary abuse risk is that free-tier social sharing widgets or the URL shortener could be embedded by malicious sites, but legitimate use overwhelmingly dominates and the vendor maintains an active presence.

ShareThis.history: Founded in 2007, ShareThis started as a social sharing button widget for websites and grew into a data platform providing audience targeting and analytics. It operates as a division of Predactiv, Inc. and has raised venture funding from Mercury Fund, Blue Chip Venture Co, Revel Partners, and Oak Hill Capital. In response to the deprecation of third-party cookies, ShareThis developed the Atlas Global ID for cookieless audience identification.
ShareThis.summary_abuse: ShareThis embeds tracking cookies and pixels on millions of publisher websites to collect browsing behavior data for targeted advertising. While its pervasive data collection raises privacy concerns, the platform is not a vector for phishing, malware hosting, or active scam operations. ShareThis provides user opt-out mechanisms, supports IAB consent frameworks, and has received Neutronian data privacy certification. The primary abuse risk is privacy-invasive data collection and behavioral profiling rather than direct fraud enablement.

Sharethrough.history: Founded in 2008 by Rob Fan and Dan Greenberg, Sharethrough developed native in-feed advertising technology. In February 2021, Sharethrough merged with District M to form a leading independent omnichannel ad exchange. In June 2024, Sharethrough was acquired by Equativ, a French ad tech company.
Sharethrough.summary_abuse: Sharethrough is an ad-tech platform powering native in-feed advertising for publishers and brands. As with all programmatic advertising networks, there is an inherent risk of ads being served on low-quality or fraudulent publisher inventory, and malvertising campaigns sometimes leverage programmatic channels. Sharethrough operates with standard ad-tech guardrails but its publisher network breadth means brand safety review is ongoing. Abuse risk is moderate and typical of programmatic ad exchanges, not exceptional.

Sharetribe.history: Founded in 2010 in Finland by Juho Makkonen and team, Sharetribe started as an open-source peer-to-peer marketplace platform built on Ruby on Rails. The company transitioned to a SaaS model and has maintained independent ownership since inception.
Sharetribe.summary_abuse: Sharetribe is a marketplace creation SaaS with no notable abuse signal. Merchants use it to run legitimate peer-to-peer or B2C marketplaces. Like any marketplace platform, individual merchant stores built on Sharetribe could theoretically be used for fraudulent listings, but this risk lies with end merchants, not the platform itself.

Sharing.history: Sharing is an AI-powered website builder based in Taiwan (web.sharing.tw). No founding details or notable ownership events are publicly documented.
Sharing.summary_abuse: Sharing is an AI website builder with no notable abuse signal. As a site-creation tool, it could theoretically be used to build fraudulent sites, but no documented patterns of abuse are known.

Sharpay.history: Sharpay is a blockchain-based social sharing and referral marketing button that rewards users for sharing content across platforms using cryptocurrency tokens. No founding year is publicly confirmed.
Sharpay.summary_abuse: Sharpay is a blockchain-based sharing widget. Its cryptocurrency reward model could theoretically incentivize spam sharing, but no documented abuse patterns are known.

Sharpen.history: Sharpen (SharpenCX) is a cloud contact center as a service (CCaaS) platform that pioneered an agent-first design philosophy. The company is headquartered in Indianapolis, Indiana and focuses on mid-market contact center customers.
Sharpen.summary_abuse: Sharpen is a B2B contact center software platform. It has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters typically target and no notable abuse signal documented.

Sharper MMS.history: Founded in 2018 by Steve and Heather Champion in Waxhaw, NC, after a marina-owning friend needed help moving from pen-and-paper operations to a digital system. The company was subsequently acquired by Greater Sum Ventures, a tech-focused private equity firm based in Knoxville, Tennessee.
Sharper MMS.summary_abuse: Sharper MMS is a niche marina management platform with no notable abuse signal. Its user base is marina operators and the product has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

SharpSpring.history: SharpSpring, LLC was founded in January 2012 in Gainesville, Florida by Rick Carlson and Travis Whitton. In August 2014 it was acquired by SMTP Inc., which rebranded itself as SharpSpring, Inc. in 2015 and went public. In September 2021, Constant Contact (backed by Clearlake Capital and Siris) acquired SharpSpring for approximately $240 million, integrating it into Constant Contact's marketing suite.
SharpSpring.summary_abuse: SharpSpring is a marketing automation platform primarily used by agencies and B2B companies. Marketing automation tools can be misused for spam campaigns, but SharpSpring's agency-focused model and pricing reduce casual abuse. No notable documented abuse incidents specific to SharpSpring have been reported publicly.

SharpSpring Ads.history: SharpSpring Ads was formerly known as Perfect Audience, a retargeting ad platform. It was rebranded after integration with SharpSpring's marketing suite, and now operates as an ad retargeting product under Constant Contact following the 2021 acquisition.
SharpSpring Ads.summary_abuse: SharpSpring Ads is a display and social retargeting platform. Programmatic ad networks carry inherent risk of malvertising; however, SharpSpring Ads primarily serves SMBs with known advertiser accounts. No specific major abuse incidents documented, with abuse risk typical of the retargeting ad-tech category.

SHE Media.history: SHE Media was founded in 2009 as BlogHer, a women-focused blog network and conference organizer. It rebranded to SHE Media and was acquired by Penske Media Corporation (PMC) in 2018.
SHE Media.summary_abuse: SHE Media is an advertising network that places ads on publisher websites. As an ad network it inherits standard ad-tech tracking risks including heavy third-party tracker density, real-time bidding data sharing, and potential malvertising exposure. It has no documented severe abuse incidents but is a typical behavioral advertising network.

SheerID.history: SheerID was co-founded in 2011 and is headquartered in Portland, Oregon. The company created an identity verification platform allowing brands to offer exclusive discounts to military, students, healthcare workers, and other identity groups by verifying through authoritative data sources. Major brands including Amazon, T-Mobile, and Spotify use SheerID.
SheerID.summary_abuse: SheerID is an identity verification platform used to confirm consumer eligibility for exclusive discounts. Its core business model specifically fights fraudulent discount claims. The platform uses authoritative data sources to verify identities rather than accepting user self-attestation, reducing the attack surface for fraud. No notable public abuse incidents documented.

Sheet Monkey.history: Sheet Monkey was created around 2020 by Smmall Inc. as a lightweight form-to-Google-Sheets connector, letting developers embed HTML forms that write directly to a spreadsheet without a backend or third-party automation service. No notable ownership changes or deprecation events are recorded.
Sheet Monkey.summary_abuse: Sheet Monkey is a small form-builder connecting to Google Sheets and has no notable abuse signal. Its free tier does not offer custom-domain hosting of arbitrary content, so it presents minimal phishing or fraud surface.

Sheety.history: Sheety was created as a developer tool to instantly convert Google Sheets into REST APIs, popular among indie developers and no-code builders. No significant ownership changes are documented.
Sheety.summary_abuse: Sheety is a developer tool that exposes Google Sheets as APIs. It has no consumer-facing hosted content surface and no documented abuse patterns.

Shelf.history: Shelf is a web server middleware framework for Dart, maintained by the Dart team at Google as part of the dart-lang organization on GitHub. It provides composable request/response handling and is the standard server-side web layer for Dart applications.
Shelf.summary_abuse: Shelf is a backend Dart server framework with no notable abuse signal; it has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

ShellInABox.history: ShellInABox was created by Markus Gutschke as an open-source web-based SSH terminal emulator. It embeds a built-in web server that serves a terminal interface via AJAX, allowing SSH access through standard HTTP/HTTPS without browser plugins. Development has been largely inactive for several years.
ShellInABox.summary_abuse: ShellInABox provides web-based terminal access to Linux servers. While it is a legitimate sysadmin tool, improperly exposed instances (open to the internet without authentication) have been targeted by attackers seeking unauthorized server access. Operators are advised to firewall the port to trusted IPs. No vendor-level abuse; risk lies entirely with misconfigured deployments.

Shepherd.history: Shepherd.js is an open-source JavaScript library for creating guided product tours, maintained by Ship Shape. It is dual-licensed under AGPL-3.0 for open-source use and a commercial license for commercial applications.
Shepherd.summary_abuse: Shepherd.js is a frontend JavaScript library for user onboarding tours. It has no server component or consumer-facing surface and no documented abuse signal.

Sherlock.history: Founded in 1980 by Phil McGuinness in New South Wales, Australia, Sherlock Software has provided real estate property management and trust accounting software to Australian agencies for over four decades. No notable ownership changes are recorded.
Sherlock.summary_abuse: Sherlock Software is a niche Australian real estate management tool with no notable abuse signal. Its user base is property management agencies and it has no consumer-facing surface exploitable for fraud.

Sherpa.history: Founded in 2014 in Sydney, Australia by Mathieu Cornillon, Ben Nowland, and Bastien Vetault, Sherpa provides on-demand and same-day courier delivery services for businesses across Australian cities. No notable ownership changes are recorded.
Sherpa.summary_abuse: Sherpa is a last-mile courier platform for Australian businesses with no notable abuse signal. Its services require business registration and are not easily exploited by fraudsters for phishing or spam purposes.

Shift4Shop.history: 3dcart was founded in 1997 in Tamarac, Florida as one of the early hosted ecommerce platforms. In 2020 it was acquired by Shift4 Payments, a public payment processing company, and rebranded as Shift4Shop. The rebrand positioned it as a free ecommerce platform for Shift4 payment processing customers.
Shift4Shop.summary_abuse: Shift4Shop is a general-purpose ecommerce platform and, like all storefront builders, can host fraudulent shops. However it is a known platform with a parent company (Shift4 Payments, NYSE: FOUR) that has standard payment-provider fraud controls. No unusually high abuse signal compared to similar ecommerce platforms.

Shine Commerce.history: ShineCommerce is a small ecommerce platform and website builder based in Hanoi, Vietnam, focused on helping businesses launch online stores. No notable founding date or ownership change events are publicly documented.
Shine Commerce.summary_abuse: Shine Commerce is a small ecommerce platform with no documented abuse signal. Its niche focus and small user base mean it is unlikely to be a significant target or vector for phishing or fraud at scale.

shine.js.history: shine.js is a small JavaScript library for creating realistic specular shine effects on HTML elements. No notable ownership events.
shine.js.summary_abuse: shine.js has no notable abuse signal; it is a small visual effects library.

Shiny.history: Shiny is a web application framework for R (and since 2022, Python), created by RStudio (now Posit). First released in 2012, it lets data scientists build interactive web apps without learning web development. Posit also offers commercial deployment via Posit Connect and shinyapps.io.
Shiny.summary_abuse: Shiny is an open-source R/Python web application framework for data apps. It has no consumer-facing abuse surface and no documented abuse signal.

ShinyStat.history: ShinyStat was founded in Italy in 1995 and established itself among Italian web operators by 1997 as one of the first browser-based real-time website traffic analytics platforms. It has remained an independent Italian analytics vendor primarily serving the Italian market.
ShinyStat.summary_abuse: ShinyStat is a web analytics tracker embedded on websites. Like most analytics tools it collects visitor data including IP addresses and behavioral data, representing a moderate tracker density. No specific documented abuse incidents are known.

ShionImporter.history: ShionImporter is a dropshipping and product importing SaaS tool. No founding details or notable ownership events are publicly documented.
ShionImporter.summary_abuse: ShionImporter is a dropshipping import tool. Dropshipping tools can be misused to create fraudulent storefronts selling counterfeit or non-existent products, though no specific incidents are documented for this platform.

ShippyPro.history: ShippyPro was founded in 2016 in Florence, Italy by Francesco Borghi and Lorenzo Rogai. In 2021 it raised a $5 million Series A from Five Elms Capital to expand globally as an all-in-one ecommerce shipping and returns platform.
ShippyPro.summary_abuse: ShippyPro is a B2B ecommerce shipping platform with no notable abuse signal. Its services require merchant account registration and focus on logistics automation rather than consumer-facing content hosting.

ShipStation.history: ShipStation was founded in 2011 in Austin, Texas by Byron Wier and Jason Hodges. In June 2014, Stamps.com acquired ShipStation for $50 million. In 2021, Thoma Bravo acquired Stamps.com, which then renamed itself Auctane. ShipStation is now the flagship shipping software product under Auctane.
ShipStation.summary_abuse: ShipStation is a multi-carrier shipping management platform used by ecommerce merchants. Its B2B focus and merchant account requirements significantly limit abuse potential. No notable abuse incidents documented.

ShipTection.history: ShipTection is a Shopify shipping protection app developed by WAM Apps, allowing merchants to offer order protection powered by InsureShip. The app is free to install with revenue sharing on protection fees charged at checkout. No notable ownership changes are recorded.
ShipTection.summary_abuse: ShipTection is a niche Shopify shipping insurance app with no notable abuse signal. Its function is adding order protection to merchant checkouts and it has no consumer-facing hosting surface exploitable for phishing or fraud.

Shipup.history: Shipup was founded in 2016 in Paris, France by Quentin Maurice and Romain Ogiela as a post-purchase order tracking and notifications platform for ecommerce retailers. In April 2026 it was acquired by ZigZag Global, a returns management company.
Shipup.summary_abuse: Shipup is a B2B order tracking platform for ecommerce retailers with no notable abuse signal. Its services are B2B-only and do not expose consumer-facing content hosting that could be exploited for phishing.

Shoefitr.io.history: Shoefitr was founded in 2010 in Pittsburgh, PA by Matt Wilkinson, Nick End, and Breck Fresen to provide 3D shoe fit matching technology for online retailers. Amazon quietly acquired the company in 2015 to reduce footwear return rates. Amazon later discontinued the service without a public explanation.
Shoefitr.io.summary_abuse: Shoefitr was a shoe size recommendation service with no notable abuse signal. The technology is deprecated and no longer in active use.

Shoelace.history: Shoelace is an open-source web component library by Cory LaViska (shoelace.style), providing accessible, framework-agnostic UI components built on the Web Components standard. It was later rebranded as Web Awesome with a commercial offering alongside the OSS core.
Shoelace.summary_abuse: Shoelace is an open-source UI component library with no notable abuse signal; it is a frontend developer tool with no consumer-facing surface.

Shogun Frontend.history: Shogun was founded in 2015 by Nick Raushenbush, Finbarr Taylor, and Damien Kan. The company raised $115M total including a $67.5M Series C in 2021, building both a drag-and-drop page builder for Shopify and Shogun Frontend, a headless commerce solution for enterprise brands.
Shogun Frontend.summary_abuse: Shogun Frontend is an enterprise headless ecommerce platform with no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B product requiring merchant accounts and has no public-facing hosting surface targeted by fraudsters.

Shogun Landing Page Builder.history: Shogun Landing Page Builder is a drag-and-drop Shopify page builder launched by Shogun Labs (founded 2015), one of the most widely-used page builder apps in the Shopify ecosystem. The company raised $115M and also launched Shogun Frontend as a headless commerce product.
Shogun Landing Page Builder.summary_abuse: Shogun Landing Page Builder is a mainstream Shopify page builder. As with most drag-and-drop builders, merchants could theoretically create deceptive landing pages, but the app itself has no documented phishing abuse and operates within Shopify's ecosystem controls.

Shogun Page Builder.history: Shogun Page Builder is the core Shopify drag-and-drop page building product from Shogun Labs, founded in 2015 and backed by $115M in VC funding from Accel, Insight Partners, and others. The platform is widely used in headless and traditional Shopify storefronts.
Shogun Page Builder.summary_abuse: Shogun Page Builder is a mainstream ecommerce page builder with no documented phishing abuse history. As a drag-and-drop tool it could be used to create deceptive merchant pages, but this risk is shared by all page builders and Shopify's platform controls provide a baseline safeguard.

Shoopy.history: Shoopy was founded in 2020 in Noida, India by Indar Kriplani, Avinash Tripathi, and Amit Kumar as a Shopify-like ecommerce platform targeting Indian MSMEs, offering Indian-language support. The company raised a $250K seed round in 2021.
Shoopy.summary_abuse: Shoopy is a small Indian ecommerce platform builder with no documented abuse signal. Its focus on Indian SME merchants and lack of anonymous signup significantly reduces fraud risk.

Shop Application.history: Shop Application was founded in 2003 in Brest, Brittany, France as a 100% French e-commerce software and website builder. The platform has served over 10,000 companies and 5,500 websites and emphasizes data sovereignty by storing customer data exclusively on French servers.
Shop Application.summary_abuse: Shop Application is a French ecommerce platform with no notable abuse signal. It targets French SMEs, stores data on French servers, and has no anonymous-signup or arbitrary content hosting features that would attract phishing actors.

Shop Pay.history: Shop Pay (formerly Shopify Pay) is Shopify's accelerated one-click checkout feature, launched around 2017. It stores customers' shipping and payment info for fast reuse across any Shopify store. By 2024 it had over 150 million users worldwide and is deeply integrated with Shopify Payments.
Shop Pay.summary_abuse: Shop Pay is a mainstream accelerated checkout operated by Shopify, a publicly traded company with strong compliance and fraud controls. It uses PCI-DSS compliant infrastructure and Shopify's extensive fraud detection. Occasionally fraudulent merchants on Shopify can exploit trusted checkout UX, but this is managed through Shopify's broader merchant screening rather than a flaw in Shop Pay itself.

Shop Pay Installments.history: Shop Pay Installments is Shopify's BNPL (buy now, pay later) product launched in 2020, offered in partnership with Affirm for US merchants. It allows customers to split purchases into interest-free or interest-bearing installments while merchants receive payment upfront.
Shop Pay Installments.summary_abuse: Shop Pay Installments is a BNPL financial product operated by Shopify in partnership with Affirm. Both companies comply with consumer lending regulations and perform identity verification and credit checks. BNPL products have faced regulatory scrutiny for encouraging overspending but no specific fraud or scam pattern unique to this product has been documented.

Shopaholic.history: Shopaholic is an open-source e-commerce plugin ecosystem for October CMS (built on Laravel), developed by LOVATA. It positions itself as the leading e-commerce solution for October CMS, offering a microarchitecture approach with modular plugins. No notable ownership changes documented.
Shopaholic.summary_abuse: Shopaholic has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted open-source ecommerce plugin that site operators deploy on their own infrastructure. The plugin itself introduces no consumer-facing abuse surface beyond what the hosting site's operator controls.

Shopapps.history: Shopapps is a small India-based Shopify Expert agency providing custom apps and services through the Shopify ecosystem. No notable founding date or ownership changes documented publicly.
Shopapps.summary_abuse: Shopapps has no notable abuse signal. It is a small Shopify partner providing apps through an established platform marketplace with built-in review and compliance controls.

Shopatron.history: Shopatron was a US-based provider of cloud order management solutions for retailers and brands, enabling distributed fulfillment from multiple store or warehouse locations. In 2015, Vista Equity Partners acquired Shopatron along with MarketLive, merging them with Fiverun to form Kibo Commerce in 2016. The Shopatron brand was retired and absorbed into the Kibo platform.
Shopatron.summary_abuse: Shopatron has no notable abuse signal. It was a B2B enterprise order management platform serving established retailers, with no consumer-facing surface suitable for phishing or scam operations. The brand is now deprecated, merged into Kibo Commerce.

ShopBase.history: ShopBase was founded in 2019 in Hanoi, Vietnam as part of OpenCommerce Group (OCG), targeting cross-border dropshipping and print-on-demand sellers. OCG raised $7M Series A in 2022 from VNG and Do Ventures, growing to serve over 86,700 sellers across 195 countries.
ShopBase.summary_abuse: ShopBase is a cross-border dropshipping platform. Dropshipping platforms carry moderate abuse risk as fraudulent or counterfeit storefronts can be set up by bad actors, though ShopBase requires merchant registration and integrates with PayPal. No specific high-profile abuse incidents are documented.

Shopblocks.history: Shopblocks was founded in 2015 in the UK as a B2B ecommerce platform. In early 2025, the company raised £2 million from NPIF II and Mercia Equity Finance and simultaneously rebranded to Symphony Commerce to signal a broader B2B commerce focus.
Shopblocks.summary_abuse: Shopblocks has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B ecommerce platform focused on commercial clients, requiring sales engagement and business onboarding, with no anonymous or consumer-facing abuse surface.

Shopbox AI.history: Shopbox AI was founded in 2020 in Ireland by Alan Gormley and Tariq Zaki, both formerly of SAS Institute, to make AI-powered ecommerce personalization accessible beyond large enterprises. The company has grown to serve retail brands across Ireland and internationally with an 11-person team.
Shopbox AI.summary_abuse: Shopbox AI has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B AI personalization tool embedded by merchant operators, with no direct consumer-facing registration or hosting surface that could be exploited for phishing or fraud.

Shopcada.history: Shopcada was founded in 2010 in Singapore as a hosted ecommerce platform focused on local sellers. As of 2024, it has only 145 live stores and a team of 1-10 employees, indicating minimal growth and possible wind-down. No notable ownership changes documented.
Shopcada.summary_abuse: Shopcada has no notable abuse signal. It is a very small Singapore-focused ecommerce platform with fewer than 200 live stores. Its limited scale and merchant-onboarding requirements provide minimal anonymous abuse surface.

Shoper.history: Shoper was founded in Krakow, Poland in 2005 by Krzysztof and Rafal Krawczyk as Devarea s.c., initially distributing software on physical CD-ROMs. The company pivoted to SaaS ecommerce and expanded through partner networks from 2008, becoming Poland's most popular SaaS storefront platform. Shoper S.A. went public on the Warsaw Stock Exchange in July 2021.
Shoper.summary_abuse: Shoper has no notable abuse signal beyond what applies to any mainstream ecommerce platform. It serves tens of thousands of Polish merchants through a managed SaaS model. Merchant account requirements and standard AUP enforcement limit anonymous storefront abuse. No documented reports of widespread scam storefront activity.

Shopery.history: Shopery was founded in 2015 in Barcelona, Spain, and is part of the Antai startup group, which also created Wallapop and other Spanish technology startups. It positions itself as a B2B marketplace and commerce platform for enterprises.
Shopery.summary_abuse: Shopery has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B enterprise marketplace platform requiring contractual engagement, with no consumer-facing or anonymous-signup abuse surface.

Shopfa.history: Shopfa is an Iranian cloud-based ecommerce platform founded around 2017 in Rasht, Gilan, Iran by Ali Aghaie and co-founders. It has supported over 2,000 online stores and positions itself as a local equivalent to Shopify for the Iranian market.
Shopfa.summary_abuse: Shopfa operates in Iran, a jurisdiction under international sanctions, which limits global payment integrations and raises baseline compliance concerns for cross-border use. As a local ecommerce platform serving Iranian merchants, it has no documented role in international fraud campaigns, but its geographic context warrants caution for users outside Iran.

ShopFactory.history: ShopFactory was founded in 1995 by Steffan Klein and Michael Veith, who formed 3D3.COM, making it one of the earliest do-it-yourself ecommerce solutions for small businesses. Based in Mulgrave, Victoria, Australia, the company has operated for over 20 years.
ShopFactory.summary_abuse: ShopFactory is a long-established niche ecommerce platform with no notable abuse signal. Its small user base and desktop software heritage mean it is not a significant vector for phishing or fraud.

Shopflo.history: Shopflo was founded in December 2021 in India by Priy Ranjan, Ankit Bansal, and Ishan Rakshit as a D2C checkout optimization platform. In April 2026, Pine Labs acquired Shopflo for INR 88 crore (~$10M USD) in an all-cash deal to strengthen its ecommerce merchant stack.
Shopflo.summary_abuse: Shopflo has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B checkout platform for Indian D2C ecommerce brands with merchant onboarding requirements. No documented cases of scam or phishing use.

ShopGate.history: ShopGate was founded in 2009 in Butzbach, Germany by Ortwin Kartmann and Andrea Anderheggen as a mobile commerce SaaS platform enabling retailers to create native mobile apps and optimized mobile websites. The company raised a $15M Series C in 2016 to expand in the U.S. market.
ShopGate.summary_abuse: ShopGate is a B2B mobile commerce platform with no notable abuse signal. Its services are merchant-facing and do not provide anonymous content hosting that could be exploited for phishing.

ShopGold.history: ShopGold is a Polish all-in-one payment processing and ecommerce platform. Limited public information is available about its founding or ownership history.
ShopGold.summary_abuse: ShopGold is a small Polish ecommerce and payments platform with no documented abuse signal. Its limited geographic reach and lack of consumer-facing anonymous hosting reduce fraud risk.

ShopHub.history: ShopHub is a Shopify growth and optimization tool. No founding details or notable ownership events are publicly documented.
ShopHub.summary_abuse: ShopHub is a Shopify store optimization platform with no notable abuse signal.

Shopify.history: Shopify was founded in 2006 in Ottawa, Canada by Tobias Lutke, Daniel Weinand, and Scott Lake, originally built as an online store for their snowboard equipment company Snowdevil. The platform launched as a product in 2006, went public on the NYSE in May 2015, and has become the dominant ecommerce platform globally with over two million merchants and significant enterprise market share through Shopify Plus.
Shopify.summary_abuse: Shopify is a large legitimate ecommerce platform used by over two million merchants. As a major hosted commerce platform with free trial periods and relatively easy onboarding, it has been documented in security research as occasionally used for scam storefronts and dropshipping fraud operations. Shopify publishes an AUP and operates an abuse reporting team, but its scale means takedown response can be inconsistent. It is not a preferred platform for phishing or malware, but fake storefront abuse (counterfeit goods, advance-fee fraud) does occur at a small fraction of overall use.

Shopify Buy Button.history: Shopify Buy Button is a native Shopify app that embeds buy functionality into external websites. It was launched by Shopify around 2015 as part of Shopify's expansion beyond its own storefront into third-party web properties.
Shopify Buy Button.summary_abuse: Shopify Buy Button has no notable independent abuse signal. It embeds checkout functionality from Shopify-managed merchant accounts, so any abuse flows through Shopify's existing merchant AUP and fraud monitoring infrastructure.

Shopify Chat.history: Shopify Chat (now Shopify Inbox) is a native live chat feature launched by Shopify to enable real-time merchant-to-customer conversations in Shopify stores. It is a free built-in product offered to all Shopify merchants.
Shopify Chat.summary_abuse: Shopify Chat has no notable abuse signal. It is a native live chat component embedded in Shopify stores, subject to Shopify's AUP and merchant account requirements. No consumer-facing or anonymous signup surface exists independent of the parent Shopify account.

Shopify Consent Management.history: Shopify Consent Management is a native Shopify app providing GDPR-compliant cookie consent banners for EU customers. It was developed by Shopify as part of its compliance feature set and is free for all merchants.
Shopify Consent Management.summary_abuse: Shopify Consent Management has no abuse signal. It is a privacy-compliance tool that adds cookie consent banners to Shopify stores, with no independent abuse surface.

Shopify Geolocation App.history: Shopify Geolocation App is a native Shopify app that recommends language and currency options based on a visitor's geographic location. It is a free utility built by Shopify to improve the international shopping experience.
Shopify Geolocation App.summary_abuse: Shopify Geolocation App has no abuse signal. It is a utility app that detects visitor location and shows language or currency recommendations, with no independent account creation or content hosting.

Shopify Product Reviews.history: Shopify Product Reviews was a free native Shopify app that added customer review functionality to product pages. Shopify eventually deprecated the app in 2023, encouraging merchants to migrate to third-party review apps as Shopify did not continue developing it.
Shopify Product Reviews.summary_abuse: Shopify Product Reviews has no notable abuse signal as a review display widget within Shopify stores. Fake review manipulation is a risk for any reviews platform, but no specific abuse campaigns are documented targeting this particular Shopify app.

Shopify Web Components.history: Shopify Web Components (Storefront Web Components) is a developer-focused SDK launched by Shopify to embed commerce capabilities into any website via web component standards. It is part of Shopify's headless commerce developer ecosystem.
Shopify Web Components.summary_abuse: Shopify Web Components has no abuse signal. It is a developer SDK for embedding Shopify commerce into custom websites, requiring a Shopify merchant account and operating under Shopify's standard compliance framework.

ShopiMind.history: ShopiMind was founded in 2013 in Marseille, France as a multi-channel marketing automation platform specifically for ecommerce merchants, offering email, pop-in, and SMS campaigns. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes documented.
ShopiMind.summary_abuse: ShopiMind is a marketing automation platform used by ecommerce merchants for email and SMS campaigns. Like all marketing automation tools, it could in principle be used for spam if access controls are weak, but no documented cases of abuse are associated with it. It operates under French and EU data protection laws.

Shopistry.history: Shopistry was founded in 2019 by Jaafer Haidar and Tariq Zabian in Canada as a headless commerce platform. In June 2021 it launched its hosted-and-headless offering and raised a $2 million seed round to position itself as a simpler alternative to custom headless commerce builds.
Shopistry.summary_abuse: Shopistry has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B headless commerce platform requiring merchant onboarding, with no consumer-facing anonymous-signup surface.

Shopiteka.history: Shopiteka was founded in 2015 in Vilnius, Lithuania by Linas Sinkunas as a small hosted ecommerce platform. The company is unfunded and operates as a solo or micro-team venture with no notable ownership changes.
Shopiteka.summary_abuse: Shopiteka has no notable abuse signal. It is a very small ecommerce platform with limited market presence. Standard merchant onboarding requirements limit anonymous abuse.

Shopix.history: Shopix is an AI-powered visual search tool for ecommerce that helps users find products via image search. No founding details are publicly documented.
Shopix.summary_abuse: Shopix is an AI visual search tool for ecommerce with no notable abuse signal.

ShopKeeper Tools.history: ShopKeeper Tools is a suite of Shopify apps for conversion optimization. No founding details or notable ownership events are publicly documented.
ShopKeeper Tools.summary_abuse: ShopKeeper Tools is a suite of Shopify conversion apps with no notable abuse signal.

Shoplazza.history: Shoplazza was founded in 2017 by Bing Xia and Junfeng Li, with headquarters in Toronto, Canada and an R&D center in Shenzhen, China. It raised $150 million in a C1 financing round and targets cross-border ecommerce merchants, particularly Chinese sellers expanding internationally.
Shoplazza.summary_abuse: Shoplazza has a somewhat elevated baseline risk compared to Western-only platforms due to its focus on cross-border ecommerce primarily serving Chinese merchants. Cross-border ecommerce platforms broadly have a documented history of counterfeit goods and scam storefronts. Shoplazza does publish an AUP and has payment processor integrations that impose some verification, but its specific enforcement posture is not publicly documented. Abuse score reflects the heightened risk of its market segment rather than documented vendor negligence.

Shoplift.history: Shoplift was founded in 2021 in New York City by Jason Boit and Austin Goldman as a no-code A/B testing platform for Shopify merchants. It focuses on theme-level split testing without requiring developer resources.
Shoplift.summary_abuse: Shoplift has no abuse signal. It is a Shopify analytics and A/B testing tool used by merchants to optimize conversion rates, with no consumer-facing or content-hosting surface.

Shopline.history: SHOPLINE was founded in Hong Kong in 2013 and grew from a three-person startup to over 2,000 employees serving 600,000+ brands across Asia. It was selected for the 500 Startups accelerator in 2014 and received investment from Alibaba Hong Kong Entrepreneurs Fund in 2016. It is the leading independent ecommerce platform in Asia.
Shopline.summary_abuse: Shopline operates primarily in Asia and serves a large base of small merchants. As with any large ecommerce platform, a small fraction of merchants may use it for counterfeit or low-quality goods sales, particularly given the cross-border commerce focus in Asia-Pacific markets. The platform does not provide anonymous hosting or content publishing, and merchant accounts require business verification.

Shoplo.history: Shoplo was founded in 2011 in Warsaw, Poland by Patryk Pawlikowski and Grzegorz Lech as an all-in-one ecommerce platform. It raised $1.3M in funding before being acquired by SumUp in 2019. In 2021, Polish ecommerce leader Shoper acquired Shoplo from SumUp for PLN 17.5 million.
Shoplo.summary_abuse: Shoplo has no notable abuse signal. It is a Polish ecommerce platform now operating under the Shoper umbrella, primarily serving Polish merchants with standard onboarding and payment requirements.

Shopmaker.history: Shopmaker is a platform for creating adult content shops selling videos and images. No founding details are publicly documented.
Shopmaker.summary_abuse: Shopmaker is an adult content storefront platform. Platforms hosting adult content storefronts can be used for counterfeit content distribution, piracy, or non-consensual content sales. No specific documented abuse incidents are known for this vendor.

Shopmatic.history: Shopmatic was founded in December 2014 in Singapore by Anurag Avula, Kris Chen, and Yen Ti Lim, with initial backing from Enterprise Singapore to help SMEs go digital. The platform expanded into India, Malaysia, and the Middle East, reaching over one million SME customers. In May 2022, Singapore-based fintech firm MatchMove acquired Shopmatic in a USD 200 million deal to create an embedded finance and ecommerce platform, with Shopmatic operating as a subsidiary under the MatchMove Group.
Shopmatic.summary_abuse: Shopmatic is an ecommerce website builder targeting SMEs in Southeast Asia and India, with no documented pattern of notable abuse. As a storefront-creation platform, it carries inherent risk of hosting fake shops, but the platform serves predominantly legitimate small merchants and requires merchant verification. The acquisition by MatchMove, a regulated fintech firm, adds oversight. No significant fraud incidents or phishing kit usage has been publicly reported for the Shopmatic platform.

Shoporama.history: Shoporama is a Danish-developed ecommerce platform founded in 2011 by Morten Blinksbjerg and Morten Vadskær. It serves primarily Danish merchants and focuses on fast, fixed-price webshop creation with local support.
Shoporama.summary_abuse: Shoporama has no notable abuse signal. It is a small Danish ecommerce platform focused on local merchants, operating under GDPR and EU data protection requirements. No documented abuse cases.

Shoppable.history: Shoppable was founded in 2011 in New York by Heather Udo, who invented and patented universal multi-retailer checkout technology. The company holds four US patents for its embeddable checkout approach, which enables consumers to purchase from multiple retailers in a single cart.
Shoppable.summary_abuse: Shoppable has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B checkout technology embedded by legitimate retailers, operating under standard payment processor compliance requirements. No documented cases of the platform being exploited for fraud.

ShopPad Infinite Options.history: ShopPad Infinite Options is a Shopify product customization app built by ShopPad Inc., founded in 2012 by Aaron Wadler in Oakland, California. The company has built over 10 Shopify apps used by more than 400,000 merchants and Infinite Options is their flagship product with over 3,000 reviews.
ShopPad Infinite Options.summary_abuse: ShopPad Infinite Options is a Shopify product options app with no notable abuse signal. It adds custom fields to product pages within Shopify's controlled ecosystem and has no consumer-facing hosting that would attract fraud actors.

ShopperApproved.history: ShopperApproved was founded in 2010 in Ogden, Utah by Scott Brandley and Garrett Pierson with the goal of helping ecommerce businesses collect more authentic customer reviews to compete with Amazon. The company made the Inc. 500 list of fastest-growing companies in the US twice.
ShopperApproved.summary_abuse: ShopperApproved is a reviews and trust-signal platform. Reviews platforms carry an inherent risk of fake or incentivized review manipulation, which has been the subject of FTC scrutiny industry-wide. ShopperApproved specifically has been cited in the ecommerce community for debates about review authenticity practices. Merchants who use it to display inflated star ratings are engaging in consumer deception, but this is not a platform-level abuse of the kind associated with phishing or fraud.

ShopPHP.history: ShopPHP is a PHP-based ecommerce software for businesses to build online stores. No founding details or notable ownership events are publicly documented.
ShopPHP.summary_abuse: ShopPHP is a PHP-based ecommerce platform with no notable abuse signal.

Shoppiko.history: Shoppiko is an India-based ecommerce platform and mobile app builder targeting small businesses in India. No notable founding date or ownership changes are publicly documented.
Shoppiko.summary_abuse: Shoppiko has no notable abuse signal. It is a small Indian ecommerce builder for SMEs with no consumer-facing content hosting or anonymous signup surface.

Shopping Feed.history: Shopping Feed (now Centric Shoppingfeed) launched in 2010 in Paris, France as a multi-channel ecommerce feed management and inventory synchronization platform. It supports over 1000 channels and has grown to serve ecommerce businesses across Europe.
Shopping Feed.summary_abuse: Shopping Feed has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B feed management tool used by ecommerce operators to synchronize product listings across marketplaces. No consumer-facing or anonymous abuse surface exists.

ShoppingFeeder.history: ShoppingFeeder is a feed management solution for online retailers, enabling product listings across multiple sales channels. No notable founding date or ownership changes are publicly documented.
ShoppingFeeder.summary_abuse: ShoppingFeeder has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B feed management tool with no consumer-facing content hosting surface.

ShoppingGives.history: ShoppingGives is a US-based B2B social commerce platform that enables ecommerce companies to incorporate charitable donations into their customers' purchases. No notable founding date or ownership changes are publicly documented.
ShoppingGives.summary_abuse: ShoppingGives has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B charitable commerce tool embedded by vetted merchant operators with no consumer-facing anonymous signup surface.

Shoppub.history: Shoppub is a Brazil-based ecommerce platform focused on advanced business rules for complex catalog and pricing scenarios. No notable founding date or ownership changes are publicly documented.
Shoppub.summary_abuse: Shoppub has no notable abuse signal. It is a small Brazilian ecommerce platform serving business clients with complex commerce requirements. No consumer-facing anonymous surface documented.

Shoppy.history: Shoppy (shoppy.gg) was founded in December 2016 as a digital ecommerce platform primarily for selling digital goods. It accepts cryptocurrency payments and offers anonymous sign-up, making it popular with gray-market sellers.
Shoppy.summary_abuse: Shoppy.gg has a well-documented abuse profile. The platform allows anonymous seller registration, accepts cryptocurrency, and hosts digital storefronts with minimal KYC enforcement. Security researchers and review sites flag it for facilitating the sale of stolen accounts, hacked credentials, malware, and fraudulent digital goods. Abuse reports submitted to the platform are frequently reported as being ignored. Multiple security scanners identify it as high-risk, and it has a very low Trustpilot score. It is a known haven for illicit digital goods trading.

Shoprenter.history: Shoprenter was founded in 2011 in Hungary, initially offering webshop development services before pivoting to a SaaS subscription model. It grew to power approximately 20% of Hungary's ecommerce market, making it the country's leading ecommerce platform. In July 2021, Polish ecommerce firm IAI (IdoSell) acquired a 51% majority stake in Shoprenter as part of its Central and Eastern European expansion strategy.
Shoprenter.summary_abuse: Shoprenter is Hungary's dominant ecommerce SaaS platform serving primarily legitimate Hungarian SME merchants. As an ecommerce storefront builder, it has theoretical exposure to fake shop creation, but the platform focuses on the Hungarian market with payment and legal requirements that reduce anonymous abuse. No documented phishing campaigns or fraud incidents leveraging Shoprenter infrastructure have been publicly reported.

Shoprocket.history: Shoprocket was founded around 2016 in the UK as an embeddable ecommerce solution allowing merchants to add a shopping cart to existing websites without rebuilding them. No notable ownership changes documented.
