Shoprocket.summary_abuse: Shoprocket is a small ecommerce widget platform used by legitimate small businesses. No documented abuse patterns; it requires Stripe or PayPal integration which provides payment-level fraud controls.

Shoproller.history: Shoproller is an Estonian website builder and ecommerce platform. No notable founding date or ownership changes are publicly documented.
Shoproller.summary_abuse: Shoproller has no notable abuse signal. It is a very small ecommerce platform based in Estonia, primarily serving local merchants with standard onboarding requirements.

Shoprunner.history: ShopRunner was founded in 2010 in the US as a membership-based free two-day shipping service connecting consumers to major retailers. In 2020 it was acquired by FedEx to strengthen the logistics giant's ecommerce ecosystem. FedEx has been integrating ShopRunner into its broader logistics and fulfillment offerings.
Shoprunner.summary_abuse: ShopRunner is a retail shipping membership network backed by FedEx. It processes real retail transactions with verified merchants and has no documented abuse pattern as a platform. Fraud risk is typical of any consumer-facing commerce network.

ShopSite.history: Founded in 1996 as iCentral, Inc. in the US, ShopSite developed one of the earliest web-based shopping cart systems. In 1998, Open Market acquired iCentral to complement its high-end Transact ecommerce engine. When the dot-com bubble burst in 2001, Open Market divested its acquisitions and the original employees bought back ShopSite, which has operated independently since.
ShopSite.summary_abuse: ShopSite has no notable abuse signal. It is a small-to-medium ecommerce platform primarily used by legitimate SMBs to build online stores. The platform requires merchant accounts and payment gateway setup, which limits anonymous abuse. No documented cases of the platform being exploited for phishing or scam storefronts at scale.

ShopStorm.history: ShopStorm is a Shopify app developer operating as Golden Rule Ventures, offering a suite of apps to extend Shopify store functionality. The company is a small independent Shopify partner with no notable acquisition or ownership changes documented.
ShopStorm.summary_abuse: ShopStorm has no notable abuse signal. It is a Shopify app platform add-on used by legitimate merchants. Being a Shopify ecosystem participant means it operates under Shopify's AUP and review framework, limiting independent abuse vectors.

ShopStyle.history: ShopStyle launched in 2007 as a fashion search engine conceived by Andy Moss while running a local e-commerce site in San Francisco. It was quickly acquired by PopSugar that same year, growing into a major fashion discovery and affiliate marketing platform listing tens of millions of products. In 2017, Rakuten-owned Ebates acquired ShopStyle from PopSugar, and it now operates under the Rakuten umbrella alongside the Ebates cashback platform.
ShopStyle.summary_abuse: ShopStyle is an affiliate and fashion search platform that aggregates retail product links. Its primary monetization is performance marketing and affiliate commissions, which carry some risk of affiliate fraud (cookie stuffing, fake referrals). It does not provide free hosting for arbitrary user content or custom domains, limiting phishing abuse. Being a Rakuten subsidiary means it operates under a large company's compliance framework. No notable public incidents documented.

Shopsys.history: Shopsys was founded in 2003 in Ostrava, Czech Republic, initially as an ecommerce agency. In 2016, the company began developing an open-source international ecommerce platform (Shopsys Framework) to serve larger merchants, and later pivoted to open-code-first strategy backed by investment.
Shopsys.summary_abuse: Shopsys has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted open-code ecommerce platform used by enterprise merchants. The platform itself introduces no consumer-facing abuse surface beyond what the hosting site operator controls.

Shoptet.history: Shoptet was founded in 2009 in Prague, Czech Republic by Tomas Krejci and Miroslav Udan through Cybergenics s.r.o. It grew to power over 46,000 online stores and 300+ employees. In 2025, European technology group team.blue acquired Shoptet, making it part of a pan-European SaaS portfolio.
Shoptet.summary_abuse: Shoptet has no notable abuse signal beyond normal ecommerce platform exposure. It serves tens of thousands of Czech and Slovak merchants through a managed SaaS model with standard onboarding and payment requirements.

Shopthru.history: Shopthru is a niche ecommerce platform with no publicly documented founding details or notable events.
Shopthru.summary_abuse: Shopthru is a niche ecommerce platform with no notable abuse signal.

Shoptimized.history: Shoptimized is a Shopify theme developed to improve store conversion rates and performance. It is sold as a one-time purchase theme with optional support packages. No notable founding date or ownership changes publicly documented.
Shoptimized.summary_abuse: Shoptimized has no abuse signal. It is a Shopify theme product with no consumer-facing hosting or anonymous signup surface.

Shoptrader.history: Shoptrader is a Dutch ecommerce platform providing online store creation and management tools for Dutch merchants. No notable founding date or ownership changes are publicly documented.
Shoptrader.summary_abuse: Shoptrader has no notable abuse signal. It is a small Dutch ecommerce platform primarily serving local merchants under GDPR and EU requirements.

ShopVOX.history: ShopVOX started as SignVOX, a business management solution for the sign industry, and rebranded to ShopVOX in 2014 to broaden its focus to custom fabricators including print, screen printing, and sign shops. It operates as TechVOX, Inc. and provides cloud-based job management software integrated with tools like QuickBooks.
ShopVOX.summary_abuse: ShopVOX has no notable abuse signal. It is a niche B2B SaaS platform for print and sign shop management used exclusively by trade businesses. Its closed, subscription-based model with business onboarding provides no meaningful anonymous or consumer-facing abuse surface.

Shopware.history: Shopware was founded in Schoppingen, Germany in 2000 by brothers Stefan and Sebastian Hamann. It became open-source with an AGPL license in 2012 when Shopware 4.0 was released. The platform is the market leader among Germany's top 1,000 B2C online shops. Shopware 6.0 was released in 2019 as a complete headless API-first rewrite of the platform.
Shopware.summary_abuse: Shopware has no notable abuse signal beyond what any mainstream ecommerce platform faces. It is a self-hostable open-source platform, so abuse characteristics depend on how operators configure and secure their installations. No documented widespread scam or phishing campaigns specifically targeting Shopware stores.

ShopWired.history: ShopWired was founded in 2012 and is operated by Platform 21 Ltd, based in Sutton Coldfield, UK. It was purpose-built for UK SME merchants as a fully hosted ecommerce platform competing against Shopify and BigCommerce in the UK market. No notable ownership changes or acquisitions have occurred; the platform remains independently operated with reported growth in merchant numbers through 2024 and 2025.
ShopWired.summary_abuse: ShopWired is a UK-focused hosted ecommerce platform used primarily by small UK merchants to sell legitimate goods. As with any storefront-creation SaaS, it could theoretically host fake shops, but it targets the regulated UK market, requires payment setup, and has no documented abuse pattern. The vendor publishes terms of service and targets small businesses rather than anonymous users. No notable fraud or phishing incidents have been publicly reported.

ShopX.history: ShopX (10i Commerce Services Pvt Ltd) was founded in 2015 in Bengaluru, India, by Amit Sharma and Apoorva Jois to digitally enable hyperlocal and Tier-2/Tier-3 city merchants in India. The company raised VC funding and briefly reached a valuation near $100 million, but filed for insolvency in August 2022 after failing to achieve sustainable unit economics.
ShopX.summary_abuse: ShopX has no notable abuse signal. It was a B2B-focused platform targeting small Indian merchants and Kirana stores. The platform ceased operations in 2022, and no documented cases of it being exploited for phishing or scam purposes exist.

Shore.history: Shore was founded in 2012 in Germany as an appointment management and scheduling software for service businesses. It is made in Germany and markets itself as GDPR-compliant.
Shore.summary_abuse: Shore has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B appointment scheduling platform used by service businesses such as salons and practices. No consumer-facing content hosting or anonymous signup surface.

Shortcodes Ultimate.history: Shortcodes Ultimate is a popular WordPress plugin by Vladimir Anokhin providing a large collection of visual UI components via WordPress shortcodes. It has been available on WordPress.org for over a decade with millions of active installs.
Shortcodes Ultimate.summary_abuse: Shortcodes Ultimate is a widely-used WordPress plugin with no notable abuse signal. It is a content-rendering library with no external hosting surface.

Shortly.history: Shortly is a URL shortener and link management tool distributed as a Shopify app for influencer marketing and social media. No founding details documented.
Shortly.summary_abuse: Shortly is a URL shortener. URL shorteners inherently carry moderate abuse risk as they can mask phishing or malware link destinations. No specific abuse incidents are documented for this particular vendor.

ShortPixel Image Optimizer.history: ShortPixel Image Optimizer is a WordPress image compression plugin and service. It was founded around 2015, likely in Romania. It offers both a WordPress plugin frontend and a cloud-based compression API. No notable ownership changes documented.
ShortPixel Image Optimizer.summary_abuse: ShortPixel has no abuse signal. It is an image optimization service for WordPress sites with no consumer-facing content hosting or anonymous signup surface that would attract abuse.

ShoutCMS.history: ShoutCMS was founded around 2008 and is headquartered in Edmonton, Alberta, Canada. It was built as an all-in-one alternative to WordPress and Squarespace, integrating a website builder, ecommerce, CRM, invoicing, and email marketing in a single hosted platform. The company has remained independent with no notable acquisitions or rebrands.
ShoutCMS.summary_abuse: ShoutCMS is a small Canadian all-in-one website builder and CMS with no documented pattern of abuse. Its ecommerce and website hosting features could theoretically enable fake storefronts, but it is primarily used by small North American businesses. No phishing campaigns or fraud incidents leveraging ShoutCMS have been publicly reported.

ShoutOut.history: ShoutOut is a multi-level marketing and affiliate tracking SaaS solution. It appears to be a small independent startup with no notable founding date or ownership changes documented publicly.
ShoutOut.summary_abuse: ShoutOut is an affiliate and MLM tracking platform. Affiliate marketing platforms inherently carry some risk of being used for click fraud or misleading commission claims, though no documented cases specifically involving ShoutOut are known. MLM structures can also facilitate pyramid-scheme-adjacent promotions.

Shoutout Testimonial.history: Shoutout Testimonial is a customer testimonial collection and display platform for businesses. It appears to be operated by a small team based in Sri Lanka. Limited public founding information is available.
Shoutout Testimonial.summary_abuse: Shoutout Testimonial is a niche testimonial management tool with no documented abuse signal. Its B2B focus and absence of anonymous content hosting make it a low fraud risk.

Show Recent Orders.history: Show Recent Orders is a social-proof pop-up widget for ecommerce sites, operated by Nice Team. No founding details documented.
Show Recent Orders.summary_abuse: Show Recent Orders is a social-proof widget with no notable abuse signal.

ShowClix.history: ShowClix was founded in 2007 by Joshua Dziabiak and Lynsie Campbell in Pittsburgh, PA as an event ticketing platform. PSG Equity took a private equity stake in the company, and it now operates under the Leap Event Technology brand.
ShowClix.summary_abuse: ShowClix has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B ticketing platform for events and venues, with transaction-based processing and no anonymous signup, reducing its appeal to fraudsters.

Showdigs.history: Showdigs was founded in 2018 in Seattle, Washington, by PropTech entrepreneur Kobi Bensimon, focused on AI-backed leasing automation for single-family rental properties. The company raised approximately $11.9 million across multiple seed rounds, including a $3 million seed in April 2019 and a $7.4 million Seed II round in February 2022.
Showdigs.summary_abuse: Showdigs is a PropTech leasing automation platform serving property managers, with no consumer-facing storefront surface and no documented abuse pattern. Its user base is professional property management companies. There are no public reports of Showdigs being used for fraud, phishing, or other malicious purposes.

Showdown.history: Showdown is a JavaScript Markdown-to-HTML converter originally created by John Fraser around 2007, based on the original Markdown Perl script by John Gruber. It became one of the most widely used Markdown converters in the JavaScript ecosystem.
Showdown.summary_abuse: Showdown is a client-side Markdown rendering library with no consumer-facing surface and no abuse signal.

Showit.history: Showit was founded around 2008 as a drag-and-drop website builder specifically targeting photographers and creative professionals. It gained a loyal following in the photography community and remains an independent company.
Showit.summary_abuse: Showit is a website builder targeting photographers. As a page builder hosting user sites, there is a baseline risk of abuse for fake business pages, but the photographer-focused niche and subscription model reduce anonymous misuse potential.

Shuttle.history: Shuttle is a website development platform operated by Devisto. No founding details or notable ownership events are publicly documented.
Shuttle.summary_abuse: Shuttle is a website development platform with no notable abuse signal.

SIDEARM Sports.history: SIDEARM Sports was founded to provide websites and digital platforms for college athletic programs. It was acquired by Learfield, a major college sports multimedia rights company, expanding its footprint across hundreds of NCAA athletic departments.
SIDEARM Sports.summary_abuse: SIDEARM Sports is a CMS and digital platform for college athletics programs with no notable abuse signal. It is a niche B2B platform serving institutional clients with no public-facing hosting surface for fraud.

SidePanda.history: SidePanda Technologies was incorporated in India in 2021 as a developer of Shopify apps including security deposit management and appointment booking tools.
SidePanda.summary_abuse: SidePanda has no notable abuse signal. It is a small Shopify app provider with no consumer-facing attack surface of interest to fraudsters.

sidr.history: sidr is a jQuery plugin for creating slide-in sidebar menus, created around 2012. The project is largely abandoned.
sidr.summary_abuse: sidr has no notable abuse signal; it is a small client-side menu plugin.

Sierra Interactive.history: Sierra Interactive is a US-based CRM and website platform for real estate professionals, founded in the 2010s; no notable acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
Sierra Interactive.summary_abuse: Sierra Interactive has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B real estate CRM platform with no consumer-facing attack surface targeted by fraudsters.

sIFR.history: sIFR (Scalable Inman Flash Replacement) was a technique created around 2004 for replacing HTML text with custom fonts using Flash. It is now entirely obsolete and abandoned since Flash's deprecation.
sIFR.summary_abuse: sIFR has no notable abuse signal; it is an obsolete Flash-based font replacement technique.

Sift.history: Sift (formerly Sift Science) was founded in 2011 in San Francisco to apply machine learning to online fraud detection. The company raised over $162M in venture funding, reaching a $1B+ valuation in 2021.
Sift.summary_abuse: Sift is a fraud prevention platform designed to detect and stop abuse, not to enable it. Its JavaScript beacon collects behavioral signals from end-user sessions to identify fraudulent activity. The data collection nature of the product means it processes significant volumes of user behavioral data, which carries some privacy considerations, but the service itself is not known to be abused by bad actors.

SIGE Loja.history: SIGE Loja is a Brazilian online store and backoffice management platform for small and medium businesses. Limited public founding history is available.
SIGE Loja.summary_abuse: SIGE Loja is a Brazilian ecommerce platform with no documented major abuse incidents. As a storefront builder it carries typical risk of fraudulent shops, but no specific campaigns are documented.

SightCall.history: SightCall is a video assistance platform for enterprises enabling remote visual support and field service. It was founded around 2012 and focuses on AR-augmented video for support workflows; no major ownership changes are documented.
SightCall.summary_abuse: SightCall has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B enterprise video support platform with no consumer-facing attack surface of interest to fraudsters.

Sigma.js.history: Sigma.js was created around 2013 at the médialab Sciences Po in Paris by Alexis Jacomy to enable in-browser interactive graph visualization for researchers. Version 2 was a major rewrite released in 2022 built on the graphology library.
Sigma.js.summary_abuse: Sigma.js is a client-side graph visualization library with no consumer-facing surface and no abuse signal.

Sign Customiser.history: Sign Customiser is a niche SaaS tool that generates product quotes and custom order forms for sign-making and print businesses embedded in online stores. Very limited public founding information is available.
Sign Customiser.summary_abuse: Sign Customiser is a niche sign-quoting tool for print shops with no documented abuse signal.

Signal.history: Signal originated from the Open Whisper Systems project by Moxie Marlinspike and Stuart Anderson, which merged TextSecure and RedPhone apps around 2014-2015. The Signal Foundation was created in 2018 by Moxie Marlinspike and Brian Acton (WhatsApp co-founder) after Acton donated $50M, providing non-profit backing for the service.
Signal.summary_abuse: Signal is an end-to-end encrypted messaging platform with strong privacy guarantees. While its encryption means content cannot be inspected, its use for criminal coordination has been noted in law-enforcement reporting; however, the overwhelming majority of use is by journalists, activists, privacy-conscious individuals, and enterprises seeking secure communication. Signal does not offer content hosting or URL shortening features that typically attract scammers. The Signal Foundation publishes an acceptable use policy.

Signalayer.history: Signalayer is a digital marketing solutions platform supporting brand engagement. No founding details are publicly documented.
Signalayer.summary_abuse: Signalayer is a digital marketing platform with no documented notable abuse incidents.

Signalize.history: Signalize is a browser push notification and marketing automation platform, likely founded around 2017; no notable ownership changes are documented.
Signalize.summary_abuse: Signalize enables browser push notifications which can be used for spam if a site that collects subscribers is compromised or the operator acts in bad faith. However, no documented large-scale abuse specifically involving Signalize is known.

SignalR.history: SignalR was created by David Fowler and Damian Edwards at Microsoft around 2011 as a library for adding real-time web functionality to ASP.NET applications. It was initially a community project and later formally adopted into the ASP.NET Core framework.
SignalR.summary_abuse: SignalR is a server-side real-time communication framework for .NET with no consumer-facing surface and no abuse signal.

Signals.history: Signals (getsignals.ai) is a cloud-based HR and pipeline management platform for managing employee workflows, tickets, and training. Limited public founding information is available.
Signals.summary_abuse: Signals is an HR management platform with no documented abuse signal. Its B2B HR focus means it has no consumer-facing hosting surface relevant to phishing or fraud.

SignalZen.history: SignalZen was founded around 2019 as a live chat SaaS that routes customer conversations directly to Slack or Microsoft Teams, eliminating the need for separate helpdesk software. The company is registered in Lithuania.
SignalZen.summary_abuse: SignalZen is a B2B live chat platform with no notable abuse signal. Customer conversations are routed to Slack or Teams and the product has no consumer-facing content hosting surface.

Signifyd.history: Signifyd was founded in 2011 in San Jose, CA to provide machine learning-based chargeback fraud protection for ecommerce businesses. It raised substantial VC funding and became one of the leading fraud protection platforms for online retail.
Signifyd.summary_abuse: Signifyd is a fraud prevention platform for ecommerce merchants, designed to detect and stop fraudulent orders. Like Sift, its data collection of customer transaction signals carries privacy considerations, but the service is not itself a vehicle for abuse.

siimple.history: siimple is a minimal CSS toolkit for building flat, clean and responsive web designs. Created by Josemi Juanes, no notable ownership changes.
siimple.summary_abuse: siimple has no notable abuse signal; it is a CSS toolkit with no server-side component.

Silex.history: Silex is an open-source web-based visual website builder created around 2012 by Alex Hoyau in France. It allows building websites directly in the browser and has remained an independent community-maintained project.
Silex.summary_abuse: Silex is a self-hosted open-source website builder. Like any tool for building web pages, sites created with it could theoretically be used for phishing, but there is no documented abuse pattern specific to Silex.

Silktide.history: Silktide is a UK-based website intelligence platform focused on accessibility and compliance checking; it has operated since the 2010s with no notable ownership changes.
Silktide.summary_abuse: Silktide has no notable abuse signal. It is a website quality and accessibility scanning tool with no consumer-facing attack surface.

Silverstripe.history: SilverStripe CMS was created in 2006 by Silverstripe Ltd in New Zealand as an open-source PHP content management system. It powers government and enterprise sites primarily in New Zealand and Australia. The company Silverstripe Ltd remains the primary maintainer and offers commercial hosting and support services.
Silverstripe.summary_abuse: SilverStripe has no notable direct abuse signal as a framework. Like any self-hosted CMS it can be compromised if left unpatched, and outdated SilverStripe installs have appeared in vulnerability disclosures, but this is a patching issue rather than a platform-level abuse vector. Legitimate-use share is overwhelming.

Simbel.history: Simbel is an Argentine ecommerce platform. Limited public information about its founding date or ownership is available.
Simbel.summary_abuse: Simbel is a small Argentine ecommerce platform with no documented abuse signal.

Simbla.history: Simbla was founded in 2013 in Tel Aviv, Israel by Ilan Zrien and Shlomi Stein as an AI-powered website builder and CRM platform with database-driven website creation. The company was part of Israel's startup ecosystem and has since evolved to focus on CRM features.
Simbla.summary_abuse: Simbla is a small Israeli CRM and website builder with no documented abuse signal. It has limited reach and no significant anonymous hosting surface.

Simla.history: Simla.com is a multi-channel CRM platform enabling businesses to manage sales and customer communications through WhatsApp, Telegram, and other messengers. It serves markets in Russia, Latin America, and other regions. No specific founding year is publicly documented.
Simla.summary_abuse: Simla is a CRM and messaging platform with no documented abuse signal relevant to phishing or fraud. Its B2B nature and messenger integration focus reduce consumer-facing risk.

Simon.history: Simon Data is a US-based customer data platform (CDP) that connects marketing data warehouses to campaign execution tools, founded around 2015. The company targets enterprise retail and media companies. No notable ownership changes or acquisitions are publicly documented.
Simon.summary_abuse: Simon Data is a B2B enterprise CDP with no notable abuse signal. Its data integration and marketing orchestration functionality operates within enterprise tech stacks without consumer-facing exposure.

Simpl.history: Simpl is an Indian fintech company founded in 2015 offering a buy-now-pay-later (BNPL) payment network primarily for Indian online retailers and consumers.
Simpl.summary_abuse: Simpl is a buy-now-pay-later fintech. BNPL platforms can be targeted for account takeover to make fraudulent purchases, but Simpl operates with KYC verification for its Indian market. No major documented abuse specific to Simpl is known.

Simple Analytics.history: Simple Analytics was founded in 2018 by Adriaan van Rossum in the Netherlands as a privacy-first alternative to Google Analytics. It intentionally collects no cookies or personal data and is GDPR compliant by design.
Simple Analytics.summary_abuse: Simple Analytics has no notable abuse signal. It is a privacy-focused analytics tool that collects no personal data and has no consumer-facing attack surface.

Simple Goods.history: Simple Goods is a lightweight SaaS platform for selling digital products online. No founding details or notable ownership events are documented.
Simple Goods.summary_abuse: Simple Goods is a digital product storefront platform. As with any platform allowing user-uploaded digital content, piracy risk exists, but no specific incidents are documented.

Simple Machines Forum.history: Simple Machines Forum (SMF) is an open-source forum software that emerged from YaBB SE around 2001-2003. It became one of the most popular self-hosted forum platforms of the 2000s and is maintained by a volunteer community.
Simple Machines Forum.summary_abuse: SMF installations can be targeted for spam if not properly moderated, a common pattern for self-hosted forum software. However, no abuse specific to SMF as a platform is documented beyond typical forum spam.

Simple.css.history: Simple.css is a classless CSS stylesheet created by Kev Quirk around 2021 that provides sensible default styling for semantic HTML with no classes needed.
Simple.css.summary_abuse: Simple.css is a static CSS file with no consumer-facing surface and no abuse signal.

Simple.ink.history: Simple.ink is a no-code website builder that uses Notion as its backend, allowing users to publish Notion pages as websites. It is a thin wrapper on Notion's API.
Simple.ink.summary_abuse: Simple.ink is a Notion-based website builder with no notable abuse signal. Content published through it inherits Notion's content policies.

SimpleHTTP.history: SimpleHTTP refers to Python's built-in http.server / SimpleHTTPServer module included in the Python standard library. It provides a minimal HTTP server for development and testing purposes and ships with Python.
SimpleHTTP.summary_abuse: Python's SimpleHTTP server is a development utility with no consumer-facing surface. It is not a vehicle for abuse and has no notable abuse signal.

Simplero.history: Simplero was founded in 2009 by Calvin Correli, who built it as an all-in-one platform for online course creators, coaches, and digital product sellers. Headquartered in New York, the company has remained independently operated by Correli and grew to approximately $10.5 million in annual revenue by 2024, serving around 700 customers.
Simplero.summary_abuse: Simplero is a niche all-in-one marketing and course-hosting SaaS targeting coaches and digital product creators. It includes email marketing and website hosting features that carry some theoretical abuse potential, but the platform serves a professional user base and has no documented phishing, fake-storefront, or spam incidents in public records.

Simplero Websites.history: Simplero Websites is the website/LMS component of the Simplero all-in-one platform for coaches and course creators. It is part of the broader Simplero product launched around 2010.
Simplero Websites.summary_abuse: Simplero Websites has no notable abuse signal. It is a learning management system for coaches and course creators with no documented history of abuse.

SimpleSAMLphp.history: SimpleSAMLphp was created by Olav Morken at UNINETT (a Norwegian university network) around 2007 to provide SAML 2.0 authentication for PHP applications. It became a widely used open-source authentication library in higher education and enterprises.
SimpleSAMLphp.summary_abuse: SimpleSAMLphp is an authentication library that has had several significant CVEs over the years exploited to bypass authentication. Vulnerabilities in unpatched deployments could allow unauthorized access but the library itself is not used as an attack tool by fraudsters.

SimpleSat.history: SimpleSat is a customer satisfaction (CSAT) survey tool for service businesses, founded around 2017; no notable ownership changes are documented.
SimpleSat.summary_abuse: SimpleSat has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B survey tool with no consumer-facing attack surface.

SimplexNoise.js.history: SimplexNoise.js is a JavaScript library implementing simplex noise algorithms for procedural generation, used in graphics and game development. It has been maintained as an open-source community project.
SimplexNoise.js.summary_abuse: SimplexNoise.js is a procedural noise library with no consumer-facing surface and no abuse signal.

Simpli.fi.history: Simpli.fi is a programmatic advertising and DSP platform founded around 2010 in Fort Worth, TX targeting political and local advertisers with unstructured data audiences.
Simpli.fi.summary_abuse: Simpli.fi is a programmatic advertising platform. Ad-tech platforms are occasionally misused for fraudulent ad placement or targeted scam campaigns, but Simpli.fi is primarily a B2B tool used by agencies and is not specifically documented as a high-abuse platform.

Simplio Upsells.history: Simplio Upsells is a Shopify app for post-purchase upsells and promotions. No founding details documented.
Simplio Upsells.summary_abuse: Simplio Upsells is a Shopify upsell app with no notable abuse signal.

Simplo7.history: Simplo7 is a Brazilian all-in-one ecommerce platform allowing merchants to create online stores quickly. Limited public information about its founding date is available; the platform has been active in the Brazilian market since at least 2014.
Simplo7.summary_abuse: Simplo7 is a Brazilian ecommerce platform with no documented abuse signal. Its B2B merchant focus and account verification reduce consumer-facing fraud risk.

simploCMS.history: simploCMS is a CMS developed by a Czech team used for various website types. Limited public information available about its history.
simploCMS.summary_abuse: simploCMS is a niche Czech CMS with no notable abuse signal.

Simplotel.history: Simplotel Technologies was founded in 2013 in Bengaluru, India, with a mission to simplify hotel technology. The platform powers hotel booking engines and websites for over 2,000 hotels across multiple countries. MakeMyTrip, India's largest online travel agency, is an investor in the company. Simplotel was recognized as the best startup in travel and hospitality for 2021 by Startup India.
Simplotel.summary_abuse: Simplotel is a hotel booking engine SaaS primarily used by legitimate hospitality businesses in India and neighboring markets. Its use case is commercial property bookings, not general-purpose content hosting, and it has no documented pattern of abuse or fraud. No phishing or fake-storefront incidents involving Simplotel have been publicly reported.

SimplyBook.me.history: SimplyBook.me was founded around 2009 as an online booking and appointment scheduling platform for service businesses globally, headquartered in Cyprus.
SimplyBook.me.summary_abuse: SimplyBook.me has no notable abuse signal. It is a scheduling and booking SaaS for service businesses with no documented history of abuse.

SimplyCast.history: SimplyCast is a Canadian multi-channel communication platform founded around 2009 offering email, SMS, and voice marketing tools; no notable ownership changes are documented.
SimplyCast.summary_abuse: SimplyCast is a multi-channel marketing platform that includes mass email and SMS features. While bulk email platforms carry inherent spam risk, no specific large-scale abuse of SimplyCast is documented.

Simplébo.history: Simplébo is a French company providing website creation and management services targeting local professionals and businesses in France, founded around 2012.
Simplébo.summary_abuse: Simplébo has no notable abuse signal. It is a French website builder for local professionals with no documented history of abuse.

SIMsite.history: SIMsite is a content management system offered by SIM Groep, a Dutch IT services company. Very limited public founding history is available.
SIMsite.summary_abuse: SIMsite is a small Dutch CMS product with no documented abuse history.

Simvoly.history: Simvoly is a Bulgarian drag-and-drop website and funnel builder founded around 2016 targeting small and medium businesses.
Simvoly.summary_abuse: Simvoly is a page builder that allows users to create and publish websites and funnels. Like all hosted page builders, sites created with it could be used for fake landing pages or phishing, though no specific large-scale abuse of Simvoly is documented.

Sinatra.history: Sinatra (in this context) is a multi-purpose WordPress theme sold at try.sinatrawp.com. It is not related to the Ruby Sinatra web framework. No founding details are documented.
Sinatra.summary_abuse: Sinatra is a WordPress theme with no notable abuse signal.

Sinch.history: Sinch was founded in 2008 in Stockholm, Sweden as a cloud communications platform provider (CPaaS). It is publicly traded on Nasdaq Stockholm and grew significantly through multiple acquisitions including MessageMedia, CLX Communications, and others.
Sinch.summary_abuse: Sinch is a CPaaS provider offering SMS, voice, email, and messaging APIs. Mass SMS and email services are commonly misused for spam campaigns. Sinch publishes an AUP and has abuse detection systems, but as a major carrier-grade platform, some volume of spam traffic is inevitable. The company has procedures for abuse reports.

Singenuity.history: Singenuity is a software platform for tour operators and attractions businesses enabling online booking of tours and activities. Limited public founding information is available.
Singenuity.summary_abuse: Singenuity is a tour booking platform with no documented abuse signal. Its B2B focus and payment processing for tours have no consumer-facing anonymous hosting surface.

Sirclo.history: Sirclo is an Indonesian ecommerce enabler company founded in 2013, helping brands sell across multiple online channels in Southeast Asia.
Sirclo.summary_abuse: Sirclo has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B ecommerce enablement platform for the Indonesian and Southeast Asian market.

Sirdata.history: Sirdata is a French data management and consent platform specialising in third-party audience data for programmatic advertising, founded around 2015.
Sirdata.summary_abuse: Sirdata is a third-party data platform for programmatic advertising. Data management and ad-tech platforms are inherently involved in user tracking, which carries privacy risk. No specific abuse of Sirdata is documented beyond the general concerns of third-party data collection.

Sirge.history: Sirge was founded in 2021 in Fort Worth, Texas as an ad tracking and marketing analytics platform for Shopify brands. The company raised $2.5M in funding.
Sirge.summary_abuse: Sirge is a Shopify analytics platform with no documented abuse signal. It collects marketing attribution data from ad campaigns and has no consumer-facing content hosting surface.

Sirv.history: Sirv is an image hosting and CDN platform offering image management and transformation for ecommerce, founded around 2014.
Sirv.summary_abuse: Sirv is an image CDN and hosting platform. Image hosting services can occasionally be misused to serve malicious content or phishing images, but no specific abuse of Sirv is documented.

Sirvoy.history: Sirvoy was created in 2008 by three Swedish brothers — Mats, Ola, and John — who built hotel management software to help their mother run her hostel in Hjo, Sweden. The platform grew to serve thousands of properties across 140 countries and is now headquartered in Dublin, Ireland. It was named Best Property Management System by HotelMinder and received top user ratings on Capterra, GetApp, and Software Advice.
Sirvoy.summary_abuse: Sirvoy is a property management and hotel booking SaaS serving small to mid-size hospitality businesses, with no documented abuse pattern. Its use is limited to hotel and accommodation operators, and it has no general-purpose content hosting or anonymous sign-up features that would make it attractive for fraud. No phishing or scam incidents involving Sirvoy have been publicly reported.

Sistem.history: Sistem is an AI-powered customization platform that develops tailored AI solutions for businesses. No founding details are publicly documented.
Sistem.summary_abuse: Sistem is an AI business customization platform with no notable abuse signal.

Site Kit.history: Site Kit is a free WordPress plugin created by Google, launched in 2018, to provide WordPress site owners easy access to Google Search Console, Analytics, AdSense, and PageSpeed Insights from within the WordPress dashboard.
Site Kit.summary_abuse: Site Kit is a WordPress plugin developed by Google with no notable abuse signal. Being a Google-backed measurement plugin, it connects WordPress sites to Google services and has no history of abuse.

Site Meter.history: Site Meter is one of the earliest web analytics services, founded around 1998, offering visitor tracking for websites via a JavaScript embed. It was once widely used but declined significantly with the rise of Google Analytics.
Site Meter.summary_abuse: Site Meter is a legacy web analytics service. It has no notable abuse signal and its declining usage means minimal security relevance.

Site Search 360.history: Site Search 360 was founded in 2017 in Dresden, Germany, by the team behind Zoovu GmbH (formerly SEMKNOX GmbH), leveraging over a decade of search technology expertise. The platform provides a customizable site-search-as-a-service solution for websites and ecommerce stores. The company remains headquartered in Dresden and has not undergone notable acquisitions or rebrands.
Site Search 360.summary_abuse: Site Search 360 is a backend site-search SaaS embedded in websites to power their internal search functionality. It has no consumer-facing hosting surface and no documented abuse pattern. The tool processes search queries submitted on client websites and does not enable arbitrary content hosting or anonymous access. No fraud or phishing incidents involving Site Search 360 have been publicly reported.

Site24x7.history: Site24x7 was launched around 2012 as a cloud monitoring service and is a subsidiary of Zoho Corporation. It provides uptime monitoring, APM, and infrastructure monitoring for websites and servers.
Site24x7.summary_abuse: Site24x7 has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B monitoring platform owned by Zoho with no consumer-facing attack surface.

SiteBooster.history: SiteBooster is a business listing distribution tool offered through the Wix App Market, helping businesses publish their details across major online directories.
SiteBooster.summary_abuse: SiteBooster has no notable abuse signal. It is a business listing tool associated with Wix.

Sitecore.history: Sitecore was founded in 2001 in Denmark, initially as a .NET-based CMS platform. It grew into a leading enterprise digital experience platform competing with Adobe AEM. In 2020, Sitecore was acquired by EQT Partners, a Swedish private equity firm. The company has since shifted toward SaaS with Sitecore XM Cloud while maintaining its on-premises offerings.
Sitecore.summary_abuse: Sitecore is an enterprise CMS and digital experience platform primarily deployed behind corporate firewalls or as a managed cloud service. As backend infrastructure software with no consumer-facing signup or content hosting surface, it has negligible abuse potential. Enterprise deployments have authentication and access controls.

Sitecore Engagement Cloud.history: Sitecore was founded in 2001 in Copenhagen, Denmark as a content management platform. It has since evolved into a leading digital experience platform (DXP) and customer data platform. Sitecore was publicly traded until being acquired by private equity firm EQT in 2020 for $1.1 billion. Engagement Cloud is the company's SaaS CDP and marketing automation offering.
Sitecore Engagement Cloud.summary_abuse: Sitecore Engagement Cloud is an enterprise B2B SaaS platform with low abuse potential. It serves large organizations for customer experience management and marketing automation with strict onboarding and compliance requirements. The platform requires contractual agreements and has SOC 2 and ISO 27001 certifications. No documented abuse patterns.

Sitecore Experience Edge.history: Sitecore Experience Edge is a SaaS headless content delivery product built by Sitecore, an enterprise DXP company founded in 2001 in Denmark. Sitecore was acquired by private equity firm EQT Partners in 2021 and delisted. Experience Edge was introduced as a cloud-native edge caching and delivery layer for the Sitecore Experience Platform, leveraging GraphQL APIs to serve content to headless JavaScript frontends at the edge.
Sitecore Experience Edge.summary_abuse: Sitecore Experience Edge is an enterprise-grade edge content delivery service for headless CMS deployments. It has no meaningful abuse signal; access requires a paid enterprise Sitecore license and is used exclusively by organizations with existing Sitecore platform investments. The product serves as a CDN/caching layer for authenticated API traffic and does not offer public self-service signup, anonymous access, or user-generated content hosting.

Sitecore Experience Platform.history: Founded in 2001 in Copenhagen, Denmark by Michael Seifert and Ole Thomsen. Grew into a leading enterprise CMS and DXP platform. Acquired by private equity firm EQT Partners in 2021. Has since acquired several complementary products (Boxever, Four51, Moosend) to build out its DXP suite. Now headquartered in San Francisco.
Sitecore Experience Platform.summary_abuse: Sitecore is an enterprise-grade CMS and DXP used by large organizations to manage web content and customer experiences. It is not a consumer-facing hosting platform; content is managed by authenticated enterprise users. Vulnerability disclosures are handled through Sitecore's security advisory program. As a backend enterprise platform, it has minimal abuse surface and no documented pattern of being used for fraud or phishing.

SiteEdit.history: SiteEdit is a Russian website builder and content management system. The platform primarily serves the Russian-speaking market. Limited public founding history is available.
SiteEdit.summary_abuse: SiteEdit is a Russian website builder with a free hosting tier, which carries moderate risk for phishing pages hosted on its subdomains. No specific documented high-profile incidents are known.

Sitefinity Insight.history: Sitefinity was originally developed as a .NET CMS platform and acquired by Progress Software in 2011. Sitefinity Insight, the analytics and personalization module, was added as a SaaS-connected customer data platform integrated into the CMS. Progress Software was founded in 1981 and is publicly traded on NASDAQ.
Sitefinity Insight.summary_abuse: Sitefinity Insight is an enterprise-grade analytics and personalization module for the Sitefinity CMS platform, developed by Progress Software. As a B2B enterprise CMS feature requiring paid licensing, its abuse surface is limited. No notable public incidents of abuse have been documented.

SiteGalore.history: SiteGalore is a white-label website builder platform that has been operating since 2001, serving ISPs, hosting companies, and service providers who resell website building capabilities under their own brands.
SiteGalore.summary_abuse: SiteGalore is a white-label website builder for resellers with no documented abuse signal. Its primary customers are hosting providers rather than end consumers directly.

Siteglide.history: Siteglide was founded in 2012 as Siteglide Limited, a private company incorporated in England and Wales. The Digital Experience Platform was built on PlatformOS, targeting agencies and SMBs. It gained traction as a migration path for Adobe Business Catalyst sites after that platform's end-of-life. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes have been publicly recorded.
Siteglide.summary_abuse: Siteglide has limited abuse signal. As a SaaS Digital Experience Platform, it allows hosting websites and customer portals under custom domains on paid plans, which creates some phishing risk. However, production deployment requires a paid subscription and the platform does not offer anonymous signup. The company maintains published terms of service and an active support team. No major abuse incidents have been publicly documented.

SiteGPT.history: SiteGPT is an AI-powered customer support chatbot platform launched around 2023, part of the wave of post-ChatGPT AI chatbot tools.
SiteGPT.summary_abuse: SiteGPT has no notable abuse signal. It is a customer support chatbot SaaS with no documented history of abuse.

SiteGround.history: SiteGround was founded in 2004 in Sofia, Bulgaria. It grew to become one of the top web hosting providers recommended by WordPress.org and serves over 2 million websites globally.
SiteGround.summary_abuse: SiteGround is a major web hosting provider. Like all shared hosting platforms, it is occasionally used to host phishing pages or spam infrastructure when accounts are compromised. SiteGround has an abuse team and responds to reports.

SiteGuard WP Plugin.history: SiteGuard WP Plugin is a free WordPress security plugin developed by a Japanese company (JP-Secure), focused on protecting against brute-force attacks and unauthorized logins.
SiteGuard WP Plugin.summary_abuse: SiteGuard WP Plugin is a security plugin with no notable abuse signal; it is designed to prevent attacks, not enable them.

Sitehood.history: Sitehood is an Israeli live chat and customer support tool for websites. Limited public founding information is available.
Sitehood.summary_abuse: Sitehood is a niche Israeli live chat widget with no documented abuse signal.

Siteimprove.history: Founded in 2003 in Copenhagen, Denmark as a digital analytics and content quality assurance platform. Grew into a leading enterprise SaaS provider serving thousands of organizations worldwide. No major ownership changes documented; remains privately held.
Siteimprove.summary_abuse: Siteimprove is an enterprise analytics and content QA platform that scans and analyzes websites for accessibility, quality, and compliance issues. It operates as a back-end scanning service with no public-facing content hosting. Abuse surface is minimal. The company maintains security certifications including ISO 27001. No notable abuse incidents documented.

SiteJabber.history: Sitejabber was founded in 2007 as a consumer review platform for online businesses. It grew to become a destination for business ratings and reviews, and launched a business-facing platform for verified reviews.
SiteJabber.summary_abuse: Sitejabber has some abuse signal as a review platform. Fake review submission by businesses to inflate ratings is a documented issue on review platforms. Additionally, phishing or fraudulent businesses may attempt to suppress legitimate negative reviews.

Siteklik.history: Siteklik is a Dutch sitebuilder for small businesses and self-employed professionals. The platform allows users to create professional websites by answering simple questions, without technical knowledge. Headquartered in the Netherlands serving the Dutch market.
Siteklik.summary_abuse: Siteklik is a Dutch website builder for small businesses. It requires business registration and operates under EU GDPR regulations. As a sitebuilder, it could be used to create fraudulent websites, but its focus on the Dutch small business market limits large-scale abuse. No notable abuse incidents documented.

SiteLock.history: SiteLock was founded in 2008 as a website security company offering malware scanning and CDN services. It was acquired by Endurance International Group and is now part of Newfold Digital.
SiteLock.summary_abuse: SiteLock is a website security service. It has no notable abuse signal and is used to detect and remove malware from websites.

SiteManager.history: SiteManager was founded in 2016 in Ghent, Belgium as a collaborative no-code/low-code web design platform for agencies and marketing teams. The company received €1.1M in growth funding.
SiteManager.summary_abuse: SiteManager is a Belgian no-code web design platform targeting agencies with no documented abuse signal. Its agency-focused B2B model reduces consumer-facing fraud risk.

SiteMinder.history: SiteMinder was founded in 2006 in Sydney, Australia as a hotel channel management and booking platform. It went public on the ASX in 2021 and is one of the largest hotel technology companies globally.
SiteMinder.summary_abuse: SiteMinder has no notable abuse signal. It is a major hotel technology company with a B2B focus and no consumer-facing attack surface targeted by fraudsters.

SiteOrigin Page Builder.history: Page Builder by SiteOrigin was created by Greg Priday and SiteOrigin around 2012 and became one of the most popular free WordPress page builder plugins, with over 1 million active installs.
SiteOrigin Page Builder.summary_abuse: SiteOrigin Page Builder is a WordPress plugin with no notable abuse signal. Like all page builders, the pages it creates could be used for phishing, but the plugin itself is not a vehicle for abuse.

SiteOrigin Vantage.history: SiteOrigin Vantage is a free WordPress theme by SiteOrigin; no notable history beyond its creation as part of the SiteOrigin product suite.
SiteOrigin Vantage.summary_abuse: SiteOrigin Vantage is a WordPress theme with no notable abuse signal.

SiteOrigin Widgets Bundle.history: SiteOrigin Widgets Bundle is a free WordPress plugin by SiteOrigin providing a library of widgets for use with SiteOrigin Page Builder.
SiteOrigin Widgets Bundle.summary_abuse: SiteOrigin Widgets Bundle is a WordPress plugin with no notable abuse signal.

SitePad.history: SitePad is a WYSIWYG drag-and-drop website builder typically offered as a white-label product by web hosting companies, particularly targeting shared hosting customers. It is developed by Softaculous Ltd in India.
SitePad.summary_abuse: SitePad is a website builder distributed primarily through hosting providers. Like any website builder with free-tier hosting, it can be used to create fake or phishing pages. However, abuse depends on the hosting partner's enforcement, not SitePad itself. No platform-specific abuse incidents documented.

Sitepark IES.history: Sitepark GmbH was founded in 1997 in Munster, Germany, specializing in CMS solutions for the public sector. The IES (InfoSite) CMS is a Java-based headless system designed for municipal websites and large editorial teams. The company also developed the atoolo PHP framework, partially released as open source. Serves approximately 9 million residents through municipal clients.
Sitepark IES.summary_abuse: Sitepark IES is a specialized CMS for German public sector organizations with no notable abuse signal. It serves municipal websites and government portals with strict security and compliance requirements. The software has no free tier or consumer-facing surface that fraudsters could exploit.

Sitepark InfoSite.history: Sitepark GmbH founded 1997 in Münster, Germany by Thorsten Liebold. Develops InfoSite CMS for German municipal and government websites. No notable ownership changes or acquisitions.
Sitepark InfoSite.summary_abuse: Sitepark InfoSite is a proprietary CMS for German municipal websites with no notable abuse signal. It is deployed on-premise by government clients, has no free tier, anonymous signup, or consumer-facing hosting features.

SitePoint.history: The SitePoint entry in Wappalyzer refers to a WordPress theme platform at sitepoint.com/wordpress, distinct from the well-known SitePoint developer content site. No notable ownership changes or major events documented for this specific product.
SitePoint.summary_abuse: This SitePoint entry refers to a WordPress theme product with no documented abuse signal. It has no consumer-facing surface that scammers would specifically target.

Siter.history: Siter is a no-code website builder developed by Designmodo, a company known for web design tools. The platform offers a Figma plugin, drag-and-drop editing, and real-time collaboration. Designmodo has a history of creating design products since its founding around 2011 by Andrian Valeanu.
Siter.summary_abuse: Siter is a no-code website builder with moderate abuse potential. It lets users create and publish websites with custom domains, including on a free tier. This combination has historically attracted phishing and scam activity on similar drag-and-drop website builders. Siter offers free subdomains, custom domain support, and SSL. The parent company Designmodo does not prominently publish anti-abuse policies. No major public abuse incidents have been specifically tied to Siter.

SiteSpect.history: Founded 2004 by Eric Vasilik as an enterprise A/B testing and optimization platform. Acquired by Monetate and rebranded as Maestro.
SiteSpect.summary_abuse: SiteSpect is an enterprise A/B testing and optimization platform with no notable abuse signal. It operates as a client-side JavaScript injection SaaS for website optimization, not a consumer-facing hosting or messaging platform. It has no free tier, no custom-domain content hosting, and is used exclusively by enterprise clients under contract.

SiteVibes.history: SiteVibes is a Chicago-based ecommerce retention and engagement platform offering loyalty, reviews, email marketing, SMS, and shoppable UGC capabilities. The company serves Shopify and BigCommerce merchants and is SOC 2 compliant, powering nearly 1 billion monthly requests.
SiteVibes.summary_abuse: SiteVibes is an ecommerce retention platform with low abuse signal. Features like reviews and UGC galleries could theoretically be used to host fake content, but the platform serves legitimate merchants under contract. SOC 2 compliance and a trust center indicate proactive security posture. Email and SMS marketing features exist but are merchant-facing rather than open to anonymous users.

Sitevision CMS.history: Sitevision AB was founded in 1998 in Sweden as a CMS and digital experience platform company. The company developed Sitevision CMS which evolved into a full DXP platform available both as self-hosted software and cloud SaaS. Sitevision is particularly strong in the Swedish public sector, serving government agencies, municipalities, and universities. The company remains independently owned and headquartered in Sweden.
Sitevision CMS.summary_abuse: Sitevision CMS is an enterprise content management platform primarily serving Swedish government agencies, municipalities, and enterprises. It has no notable abuse signal. The platform requires a paid license and is deployed either on-premise or in Sitevision's European-hosted cloud. Public self-service signup is not available, and the product is used exclusively by vetted organizations. Sitevision holds ISO/IEC 27001:2022 certification and operates under strict EU GDPR regulations.

SiteW.history: Founded in 2007 in France as a website building service. No notable ownership changes or acquisitions have occurred since founding.
SiteW.summary_abuse: SiteW is a French website builder offering a free tier and custom domain support, features commonly associated with phishing and scam landing pages. As a 19-year-old French company subject to EU regulations, it maintains standard abuse reporting channels and provides GDPR compliance tools including cookie consent management. No notable public incidents of widespread abuse on the platform have been widely documented. The platform hosts sites on its own infrastructure, and its longevity suggests established operational practices.

SiteWrench.history: SiteWrench is a hosted CMS developed by Speak Creative, a marketing agency founded in 1999 in Memphis, Tennessee. SiteWrench has been available since approximately 2015 as a managed CMS platform targeting zoos, museums, attractions, churches, and small businesses. It remains a product line within Speak Creative with no known acquisitions or ownership changes.
SiteWrench.summary_abuse: SiteWrench is a managed hosted CMS requiring sales engagement for signup, with no self-serve free tier or anonymous signup. Its client base consists primarily of established organizations (museums, zoos, churches, medical) vetted through a direct sales process. The managed support model gives the vendor direct oversight of hosted content. No documented history of significant abuse incidents or patterns was found.

SiteWright.history: SiteWright is a niche website builder based in Atlanta, Georgia, USA, focused exclusively on fitness and wellness businesses. Public records do not reveal a specific founding year or notable corporate events. The platform serves personal trainers, gyms, and wellness professionals seeking a purpose-built online presence.
SiteWright.summary_abuse: SiteWright is a niche fitness website builder targeting professional trainers and gyms, with no documented abuse pattern. Its narrow vertical focus and professional user base limit fraud exposure. No phishing or fake-storefront incidents involving SiteWright have been publicly reported.

Sitey.history: Sitey was a drag-and-drop website builder that operated as a brand within the Web.com portfolio, ultimately owned by Newfold Digital (formerly Endurance International Group). The Sitey domain was registered in 2002, and the brand was actively marketed by at least 2021. In January 2026, Sitey was officially merged into Network Solutions; sitey.com now redirects to networksolutions.com/sitey. No standalone product remains.
Sitey.summary_abuse: Sitey was a drag-and-drop website builder with a free tier and custom domain support — characteristics commonly exploited by scammers to host fake storefronts and phishing pages. As a relatively small platform within the Web.com/Network Solutions portfolio, it had an active abuse reporting channel (sitey.com/abuse) and benefited from Newfold Digital's anti-abuse infrastructure. No major public abuse incidents specific to Sitey were documented. The platform has since been subsumed into Network Solutions.

Sitoo.history: Sitoo was founded around 2015 in Sweden as an ecommerce platform before pivoting to a unified commerce and POS platform. It now serves 300+ enterprise retailers including Levi's, Puma, and Paul Smith across 27 countries with offices in Sweden, Denmark, the Netherlands, and the UK. The company achieved MACH certification in 2022 and earned SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001 certifications. No notable ownership changes or acquisitions have occurred.
Sitoo.summary_abuse: Sitoo is a B2B POS and unified commerce platform for enterprise retailers. It has no meaningful abuse signal as a backend SaaS platform with no consumer-facing free tier, no custom domain hosting, and no anonymous signup options. The company maintains a responsible disclosure policy and publishes SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001 certifications. No documented patterns of abuse or fraud are associated with the platform.

Sivuviidakko.history: Sivuviidakko is a Finnish CMS product developed by Liana Technologies, a digital marketing and communications software company headquartered in Finland. The product was part of Liana Cloud's suite of marketing and communications tools. Liana Technologies has been operating for over 20 years and achieved ISO 27001 certification. The standalone sivuviidakko.fi domain has since been redirected to Liana Technologies' main site.
Sivuviidakko.summary_abuse: Sivuviidakko has minimal abuse signal as a CMS product offered through Liana Technologies. Its parent company Liana Technologies maintains ISO 27001 certification and GDPR-compliant data practices. As a CMS platform sold primarily to Finnish and European enterprise customers, abuse risk is low with standard content management controls. No notable abuse incidents have been reported.

Siweb.history: Founded in 2006 in Vigo, Spain by Raul Garcia as an AI-based website builder and digital solutions provider. Grew to 300+ employees with offices in Vigo and Las Palmas. Won Best Digital Transformation Project 2024 and Innovation and Digital Transformation 2025 awards. Official sponsor of RC Celta football club. Expanding to Portugal, Italy, and Chile in 2026.
Siweb.summary_abuse: Siweb is a Spanish AI website builder and digital solutions provider serving SMEs primarily in Spain. As a sales-gated platform with EU GDPR compliance and ENS security certification, its abuse surface is limited. The platform requires direct sales engagement for onboarding, reducing anonymous abuse risk. No notable public incidents of abuse have been documented.

Sixads.history: Sixads was developed by Sixads, UAB (Lithuania) as a Shopify app for automating Facebook, Instagram, and Google ads for online stores. The product first appeared around 2017 and was listed on the Shopify App Store with a 4.5-star rating. The company remained very small with only 2 employees and modest annual revenue of approximately 85,000 EUR in 2024. As of late 2025 to 2026, the sixads.net website is unreachable and the app is no longer listed on the Shopify App Store, indicating the product is dormant.
Sixads.summary_abuse: Sixads was a small Shopify ad automation app with no documented abuse signal. During its active period it operated as a standard ad-management tool connecting Shopify stores to Facebook and Google Ads APIs, with no consumer-facing surface for hosting content or facilitating scams. The product is now dormant with its website unreachable and app removed from the Shopify App Store, so the abuse risk is effectively zero.

SixCMS.history: Six Offene Systeme GmbH was founded in 1991 in Stuttgart, Germany. SixCMS was developed as a headless content management system targeting German enterprises and public sector organizations. The product supports API-based content delivery, custom data structures, and encrypted fields. Customers include German federal states, CEWE, and Klett. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes are publicly recorded.
SixCMS.summary_abuse: SixCMS has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise-grade on-premise CMS deployed behind organizational firewalls for German government and corporate clients. The software offers no public hosting, no anonymous signup surface, and requires a licensed enterprise deployment. No documented incidents of SixCMS being used in abusive contexts have been reported.

Sixshop.history: Sixshop was launched around 2003 (domain registered October 2003) by Sellerhub Inc. in Seoul, South Korea. It began as a shopping mall creation system and evolved into an AI-powered website and ecommerce builder with multi-channel marketplace management. In 2022 Sixshop Pro was introduced with an AI web builder. The platform serves over 200,000 brands primarily in South Korea. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes have occurred.
Sixshop.summary_abuse: Sixshop is a Korean ecommerce and website builder SaaS operating primarily in South Korea. Store builders can theoretically be used for fraudulent storefronts, but Sixshop operates in a regulated Korean ecommerce environment requiring real identity and business registration verification. The platform publishes terms of service and a privacy policy. No notable public incidents of widespread abuse have been documented. Abuse risk associated with the platform itself is low.

Sizebay.history: Sizebay was founded in 2011 in Joinville, Santa Catarina, Brazil, as a virtual fitting room and size recommendation platform for fashion e-commerce, serving customers like Nike, Decathlon, and Levi's.
Sizebay.summary_abuse: Sizebay has no notable abuse signal. It is a niche B2B SaaS tool for virtual fitting rooms embedded on fashion retailers' websites, with no consumer-facing surface fraudsters typically target.

Sizekick.history: Sizekick was founded in summer 2022, partnering with German textile testing institute Hohenstein, which invested 1.3 million euros and contributed 75 years of sizing expertise to the AI-based size recommendation solution.
Sizekick.summary_abuse: Sizekick has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B SaaS sizing tool for fashion e-commerce, with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters typically target.

SizeMe.history: SizeMe AI was founded in 2022 in Buenos Aires, Argentina, as an AI-powered size recommendation platform for fashion e-commerce retailers.
SizeMe.summary_abuse: SizeMe has no notable abuse signal. It is a niche B2B SaaS tool for size recommendations embedded on retailer websites, with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters typically target.

Sizey.history: Sizey is an AI-powered sizing and apparel management SaaS for online fashion brands. No founding details are publicly documented.
Sizey.summary_abuse: Sizey is a fashion sizing tool with no notable abuse signal.

Sizmek.history: Sizmek was founded in 2000 (as DG FastChannel / Digital Generation) and evolved into a major digital ad-serving and analytics platform. Amazon acquired most of Sizmek's assets in 2019 after Sizmek filed for bankruptcy. Its ad-serving and DCO technology was integrated into Amazon Advertising, effectively ending the standalone Sizmek brand.
Sizmek.summary_abuse: Sizmek was an ad-tech platform serving digital display advertising. As a heavy ad-tech player it embedded multiple tracking technologies into publisher pages. It was not known as a phishing or fraud infrastructure platform, but its ad-serving SDKs could carry third-party tracker payloads. The platform is now largely defunct after the Amazon acquisition.

Skai.history: Founded in 2006 in Israel as Kenshoo, it became a leading search and social media advertising management platform backed by Sequoia Capital, Bain Capital Ventures, and other investors. The company rebranded to Skai in July 2021 to reflect its expanded focus on commerce media and retail advertising.
Skai.summary_abuse: Skai is an enterprise marketing activation platform used by major brands and agencies. It operates in the ad-tech space and collects campaign performance data, but there is no notable abuse signal specific to Skai. Its access is gated behind enterprise sales contracts, limiting exposure to fraudulent actors.

Skedify.history: Founded in 2014 in Ghent, Belgium by Christophe Thelen and Arne Bassez as an enterprise appointment scheduling platform. Skedify was acquired by Norwegian video conferencing company Pexip in November 2021 and rebranded as Pexip Engage.
Skedify.summary_abuse: Skedify (now Pexip Engage) has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise appointment scheduling tool sold to large organizations and financial institutions, with no anonymous sign-up or consumer-facing hosting surface that fraudsters target.

Skeeks.history: SkeekS CMS is an open-source content management system built on the Yii2 PHP framework, maintained by a small team or solo developer, primarily used in Russian-speaking markets.
Skeeks.summary_abuse: SkeekS CMS has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted PHP CMS with a small niche user base, and no consumer-facing hosted surface fraudsters typically target.

Skeep.history: Skeep is a post-2022 AI-powered e-commerce quiz and personalization SaaS platform targeting Shopify and other online retailers.
Skeep.summary_abuse: Skeep has no notable abuse signal. It is a quiz-based personalization tool embedded on e-commerce sites, with no notable history of misuse.

Skeepers.history: Founded in 2012 in Marseille, France as Net Reviews (operator of Avis Vérifiés). In 2019 the group rebranded to Skeepers and expanded via acquisitions of MediaTech-cx and Teester, growing into a customer engagement and feedback platform group.
Skeepers.summary_abuse: Skeepers has no notable abuse signal. It is a customer feedback and engagement SaaS primarily sold to European retailers and brands. The platform collects consumer reviews and satisfaction data under GDPR, with no consumer-facing hosting surface associated with fraud.

Sketchanet.history: Sketchanet is a code-free website creation system. No founding details or notable events are publicly documented.
Sketchanet.summary_abuse: Sketchanet is a website builder with no notable abuse signal.

Skilldo.history: Skilldo is a PHP and MySQL/MariaDB-based CMS developed by a Vietnamese team, positioned as an open-source content management system for web projects.
Skilldo.summary_abuse: Skilldo has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted PHP CMS with a small regional user base, primarily used in Vietnam.

Skilljar.history: Founded in 2013 in Seattle, Washington as Everpath, later rebranded to Skilljar. The company raised a $33 million Series B from Insight Partners in 2020 and was acquired by Gainsight in April 2025.
Skilljar.summary_abuse: Skilljar has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise B2B customer training LMS used by companies like DocuSign and Twilio, with no consumer-facing hosting surface that fraudsters target.

Skimlinks.history: Skimlinks was founded in 2007 in London as an affiliate link monetization platform, automatically converting outbound links on publisher pages into tracked affiliate links. In 2020 it was acquired by Connexity, and then in 2021 Connexity was acquired by Taboola, the native advertising giant, making Skimlinks a Taboola subsidiary.
Skimlinks.summary_abuse: Skimlinks automatically rewrites outbound links on publisher pages to tracked affiliate URLs, which means it injects its own tracking into every page that uses it. It is classified as heavy-tracker adjacent. There is no notable phishing or fraud-infrastructure abuse, but the automatic link rewriting creates an opaque user experience. Taboola/Skimlinks publishes an AUP and GDPR DPA.

Skio.history: Founded in 2021 by Kennan Frost, backed by Y Combinator (S20), as a Shopify subscription management platform. Skio raised $3.7M in late 2021 and was acquired by Recharge for $105 million in April 2026.
Skio.summary_abuse: Skio has no notable abuse signal. It is a subscription billing platform for Shopify stores, used exclusively by legitimate merchants, with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters target.

Skiperformance.history: Skiperformance was founded in 2014 by online travel professionals with over 20 years of combined experience. The company operates across Norway, Italy, and France with a partner presence in Austria, providing ecommerce and ticketing solutions specifically for the ski resort and lift ticket industry in Europe.
Skiperformance.summary_abuse: Skiperformance is a vertical SaaS for ski resort lift ticket and pass sales, serving professional hospitality operators. Its narrow industry focus and B2B ticketing model provide minimal abuse surface. No fraud incidents, phishing campaigns, or illicit use of Skiperformance infrastructure have been publicly documented.

Skitter.history: Skitter is a jQuery-based image slider library that has seen minimal development activity and is considered effectively abandoned, with no notable releases in recent years.
Skitter.summary_abuse: Skitter has no abuse signal. It is a small client-side JavaScript slider library with no server-side surface.

Skolengo.history: Skolengo is an education management SaaS product from Kosmos Education, a French company founded in 1998 as a specialist in digital transformation for the education sector. The Skolengo product was launched around 2019 and is deployed in over 3,600 educational institutions across 70 countries.
Skolengo.summary_abuse: Skolengo has no notable abuse signal. It is an education management platform used by accredited schools and institutions, with access controlled by institutional administrators.

Skool.history: Founded in 2019 by Sam Ovens (CEO) and Daniel Kang (CTO) as a community platform combining online courses, gamification, and community features for knowledge entrepreneurs.
Skool.summary_abuse: Skool has a low but non-zero abuse risk. It is a community platform used primarily by online course creators and info-product sellers, a market that has seen some association with high-pressure sales tactics and low-value coaching schemes, though the platform itself has no notable abuse incidents. Users can create paid communities, and the platform is used by some marketers who run aggressive upsell funnels.

Skrollr.history: Skrollr is a stand-alone JavaScript parallax scrolling library created by Alexander Prinzhorn around 2012. The author officially stopped active development around September 2014 and warned users that mobile browser compatibility issues would likely surface. The project remains available on GitHub but is abandoned.
Skrollr.summary_abuse: Skrollr has no abuse signal. It is a client-side JavaScript library with no server-side surface and no consumer-facing features that fraudsters could exploit.

Sky Pilot.history: Sky Pilot is a Shopify app for selling and delivering digital products (downloads, streaming content) to customers after purchase.
Sky Pilot.summary_abuse: Sky Pilot has no notable abuse signal. It is a digital delivery app for legitimate Shopify merchants, used to distribute purchased digital products like ebooks and videos.

Sky-Shop.history: Sky-Shop was founded in 2009 by Przemyslaw Matoga, also known for his phpBB2 modifications, and is headquartered in Krakow, Poland. The platform grew to serve over 90,000 online stores in Poland, specializing in ecommerce and dropshipping integrations with Polish and international marketplaces including Allegro, eBay, and Amazon.
Sky-Shop.summary_abuse: Sky-Shop is a Polish ecommerce and dropshipping SaaS primarily serving small Polish merchants. Ecommerce platforms carry inherent risk of fake-storefront creation, but Sky-Shop operates in the Polish regulated market with marketplace integrations that impose seller verification. No significant phishing campaigns or fraud incidents specifically linked to Sky-Shop infrastructure have been publicly reported.

Skyflow.history: Founded in 2019 by Anshu Sharma and Prakash Khot, two former Salesforce executives. Skyflow raised a $7.5M seed in 2020, $17.5M Series A in 2020, and $45M Series B in October 2021, becoming a notable privacy-vault-as-API company targeting fintech and healthtech.
Skyflow.summary_abuse: Skyflow has no abuse signal. It is a data privacy vault API designed specifically to protect sensitive data; its core product purpose is to prevent data exposure rather than facilitate it.

SkyGlue.history: SkyGlue is a Google Analytics add-on for automated event tracking and visitor journey analysis, founded around 2011 in Fremont, California. It has provided analytics tooling for web developers for over a decade.
SkyGlue.summary_abuse: SkyGlue is a website analytics tool that tracks visitor interactions and collects behavioral data. Like all analytics trackers it collects user data, but has no notable phishing or fraud abuse signal beyond standard privacy concerns.

SkyVerge.history: SkyVerge was founded around 2013 in the US as a WooCommerce plugin development company, building payment, shipping, and account management extensions. The company was acquired by Bain Capital-backed StellarWP (formerly Liquid Web's Managed WooCommerce focus), which consolidated multiple WooCommerce extension brands.
SkyVerge.summary_abuse: SkyVerge develops WooCommerce extensions. As a plugin/extension provider with no end-user consumer surface it has no notable abuse signal. Legitimate use vastly dominates.

Slaask.history: Slaask is a customer messaging SaaS built on top of Slack, allowing companies to handle customer service conversations within Slack's interface.
Slaask.summary_abuse: Slaask has no notable abuse signal. It is a niche live chat tool that routes messages through Slack, used by small businesses for customer support.

Slapform.history: Slapform is a lightweight form-backend SaaS product that stores HTML form submissions in the cloud and emails them to the form owner.
Slapform.summary_abuse: Slapform is a form-backend service. Form backends can receive spam submissions but Slapform itself is not a vehicle for phishing or scam infrastructure. No documented abuse incidents.

Slate.history: Technolutions was founded in 1994 and launched Slate in 2000 as a CRM platform for higher education. Slate has grown to serve over 2,100 colleges, universities, and secondary schools worldwide for admissions, enrollment management, and advancement.
Slate.summary_abuse: Slate by Technolutions has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise CRM sold exclusively to accredited educational institutions for internal admissions and enrollment management.

Sleeknote.history: Sleeknote was founded in 2014 in Denmark as a website popup and lead-capture SaaS tool targeting ecommerce businesses. It remains independently owned with a focus on converting website visitors through contextual popups.
Sleeknote.summary_abuse: Sleeknote is a popup/lead-capture tool used by ecommerce marketing teams. Its popup nature means some deployments may exhibit pushy UX (exit-intent popups, email-gate patterns), hence a moderate dark-pattern score. No documented fraud or scam abuse; it has no consumer-hostile direct surface.

Sleekplan.history: Sleekplan is a small SaaS product feedback and roadmap tool launched around 2020, primarily serving individual developers and small software teams.
Sleekplan.summary_abuse: Sleekplan has no notable abuse signal. It is a feedback widget embedded in SaaS products with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters target.

Slice.history: Slice (formerly MyPizza.com) is a US-based food ordering platform founded around 2010, focused specifically on independent pizzerias. The company has raised significant venture funding and grown to serve thousands of independently-owned pizza restaurants across the US.
