Store Vantage.summary_abuse: Store Vantage has minimal abuse signal. As a niche small business scheduling and CRM platform, its abuse potential is limited to occasional unsolicited appointment reminders or marketing emails. No documented incidents of significant abuse have been reported.

Storearmy.history: Storearmy is a DIY ecommerce platform created by Waltfox Ventures Private Limited, based in Ahmedabad, Gujarat, India. Waltfox Ventures is part of the Servoday Group, a manufacturing and export conglomerate with over 40 years of history. Storearmy launched in the mid-2010s as a hosted website builder and online store system with integrated payment gateways and logistics, targeting Indian small and medium businesses.
Storearmy.summary_abuse: Storearmy is a small Indian ecommerce website builder serving primarily small and medium businesses. The platform allows users to create online stores with integrated payment gateways and logistics, and offers a free tier with no time limit. Custom domain support means stores can appear as legitimate businesses, which creates some surface for fake storefronts. However, all paid plans are entirely sales-gated (no public pricing), requiring direct contact with the company, which provides some human screening. There are no notable public incidents of Storearmy being widely used for scams or fraud. The company publishes an Acceptable Use Policy and has terms of service governing platform use.

Storeden.history: Storeden was founded in 2013 in Villorba, Veneto, Italy, as a cloud multi-channel ecommerce platform targeting Italian small and medium businesses. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes.
Storeden.summary_abuse: Storeden is an Italian ecommerce platform with a small market footprint primarily in Italy. Like any hosted ecommerce builder, its storefronts could in principle host scam shops, but there is no documented pattern of widespread abuse. The vendor maintains standard ecommerce AUP policies.

Storefront.history: Storefront (branded as OrderEazi) is a South African ecommerce SaaS platform developed by Warp Development. Founded in 2004 by Rudi Mostert and Adrien Erasmus, the company started building custom B2B ecommerce solutions before launching its fully SaaS product in 2019. It remains an independent, privately held company serving primarily the South African market.
Storefront.summary_abuse: OrderEazi/Storefront is a South African ecommerce SaaS platform that enables businesses to build online stores and manage orders. As a hosted platform for legitimate merchants, it offers storefront hosting, checkout, and payment processing features that could theoretically be used for fake storefronts. However, signup requires sales engagement rather than anonymous self-service, and the platform's focus on the B2B South African market limits its attractiveness for large-scale abuse. No notable public incidents of abuse have been documented.

Storefront UI.history: Created by the Vue Storefront (now Alokai) team as an open-source UI component library and design system for eCommerce storefronts. Evolved from a Vue-only library to a framework-agnostic solution also supporting React. Built on Top of Tailwind CSS with a focus on accessibility (WCAG AA compliant) and performance.
Storefront UI.summary_abuse: Storefront UI has no notable abuse signal. It is a frontend UI component library distributed as npm packages for building eCommerce storefronts. It does not host user content, provide server-side functionality, offer custom domains, or expose any consumer-facing hosting features that fraudsters could exploit.

StoreHippo.history: Founded 2014 by Rajiv Aggarwal in Gurgaon, India. StoreHippo is a bootstrapped ecommerce platform (no outside funding) that grew to $94.6M ARR by 2024. No notable ownership changes, acquisitions, or deprecation events since founding.
StoreHippo.summary_abuse: StoreHippo has limited documented abuse signal. As a hosted ecommerce platform with custom-domain support and free SSL, it could theoretically be used for fake storefronts, but no notable patterns of widespread phishing or scam abuse have been publicly reported. The platform operates primarily in India with standard AUP enforcement; there are no known high-profile incidents of abuse or takedown failures.

Storeify Store Locator.history: Storeify Store Locator is a Shopify app that adds store locator functionality to online stores. It offers a freemium SaaS model and requires a Shopify store for integration.
Storeify Store Locator.summary_abuse: Storeify Store Locator is a Shopify app adding store locator maps to online stores. It requires a Shopify store and business registration. As a simple map embed app within Shopify's ecosystem, it has minimal abuse surface. No notable abuse incidents documented.

Storeino.history: Founded in 2015 in Agadir, Morocco by Abdelkarim Mamarch and Abdellah Moudnib as an ecommerce platform focused on cash-on-delivery payments for the African market. The platform now serves over 45,000 merchants. No notable ownership changes or deprecation events since founding.
Storeino.summary_abuse: Storeino is a regional ecommerce SaaS platform primarily serving merchants in North Africa with a focus on cash-on-delivery payments. It has no well-documented public history of notable abuse. As a hosted storefront platform, it could theoretically be used for fake storefronts or scam pages, but its regional focus and COD-payment model limit its appeal for large-scale global scam operations. The platform provides SSL certificates and includes checkout security features. No specific public abuse incidents or security research reports were identified. The vendor does not appear to have a published bug bounty or abuse-reporting program.

Storenvy.history: Founded around 2011 by Jon Crawford as an indie-brand ecommerce marketplace. The company was initially accepted into Y Combinator but was later removed; it subsequently raised approximately $5 million from Kleiner Perkins and other investors in 2013. Storenvy has operated independently since as Storenvy Online Services LLC. No notable ownership changes or deprecation events.
Storenvy.summary_abuse: Storenvy is an indie-brand ecommerce marketplace and store-building platform. As a marketplace hosting independent sellers, there is some inherent risk of counterfeit or IP-infringing product listings, though no significant public incidents have been documented. The platform uses Stripe and PayPal for payment processing, supports custom domains on paid plans, and requires seller registration. Storenvy publishes terms of service, a creative rights policy, and maintains a responsible disclosure program for security issues. Abuse exposure is low relative to larger marketplaces.

Storeplum.history: Storeplum is a no-code ecommerce website builder targeting small businesses and entrepreneurs; no notable ownership changes or deprecation events found.
Storeplum.summary_abuse: Storeplum is a small ecommerce platform builder with no notable abuse signal. As a niche platform used primarily by small businesses, it has limited exposure as an abuse vector compared to larger platforms.

StorePoint.history: StorePoint was founded in 2015 by Jake in Toronto, Canada, to address the problem of generic store locator widgets that looked out of place on branded websites. The platform has grown to serve brands including SodaStream, Cult Gaia, and Kung Fu Tea across six continents without taking any external funding.
StorePoint.summary_abuse: StorePoint is a store locator widget SaaS with no content hosting or user-generated surface. Its function is to display maps of physical store locations embedded on client websites. This presents no meaningful abuse vector for phishing, fraud, or spam. No abuse incidents have been documented.

StoreRocket.history: Founded circa 2018 as StoreRocket Ltd, a store locator SaaS widget for websites. Compatible with most website platforms including WordPress, Shopify, and Squarespace. No notable ownership changes or deprecation events.
StoreRocket.summary_abuse: StoreRocket is a hosted store locator widget for websites; it has no meaningful abuse signal. The service lets businesses display physical locations on an interactive map and does not host user-generated content or offer anonymous signup. Uses Google Analytics for basic telemetry. No documented incidents of the platform being used for phishing, scams, or malware distribution.

stores.jp.history: stores.jp is a Japanese ecommerce platform founded in 2008 in Tokyo that enables small businesses to create online stores. The company has raised $22M from investors including Google, Z Venture Capital, and ZOZO.
stores.jp.summary_abuse: stores.jp is a Japanese ecommerce hosting platform. Like any storefront-hosting service it could potentially host fraudulent stores, but it is primarily used by legitimate Japanese small businesses with no documented systematic abuse pattern.

StorifyMe.history: StorifyMe was founded in 2019 in Munich, Germany, by Nemanja Popovic and Aleksandar Micic as a storytelling SaaS platform for web and mobile stories. No notable ownership changes since founding.
StorifyMe.summary_abuse: StorifyMe is a niche storytelling platform with no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B content tool and does not expose free-tier custom domain hosting or anonymous signup features that would make it attractive for phishing or scam operations.

Storrea.history: Storrea was founded in December 2015 by Ashfaq Rahman and co-founders in Dhaka, Bangladesh, as a bootstrapped SaaS ecommerce platform targeting the Bangladeshi market with no external funding raised.
Storrea.summary_abuse: Storrea has no notable abuse signal. It is a small bootstrapped ecommerce platform primarily serving the Bangladeshi market, with limited consumer-facing exposure that would make it attractive to fraudsters.

Storyblok.history: Storyblok was founded in 2017 by Dominik Angerer and Alexander Feiglstorfer in Linz, Austria. The company raised $8.5M Series A in 2021, $47M Series B in 2022, and $80M Series C in 2024, growing to become one of the leading headless CMS platforms with over $138M raised.
Storyblok.summary_abuse: Storyblok has no notable abuse signal. It is a headless CMS used by developers and marketing teams, without consumer-facing features that would make it an attractive target for phishing or fraudulent content hosting.

Storybook.history: Storybook was originally created by Kadira in 2016 as React Storybook, then open-sourced. It was later renamed Storybook and expanded to support Vue, Angular, and other frameworks. Chromatic, a visual testing company, became the primary commercial steward and primary maintainer of the project.
Storybook.summary_abuse: Storybook has no abuse signal. It is a developer tool for building and documenting UI components in isolation, with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

Storylane.history: Storylane was founded in 2021 by Akash Bansal and Nalin Senthamil in San Francisco, and went through Y Combinator's program. The company raised seed funding and grew to 55 employees by 2024 with approximately $1.9 million in annual revenue. Storylane acquired PreSkale to expand its product capabilities.
Storylane.summary_abuse: Storylane is a B2B SaaS for creating interactive product demos targeting sales and marketing teams. Its content consists of product screenshots and guided tours created by business users, with no consumer-facing hosting or anonymous sign-up exposure. No fraud or phishing incidents involving Storylane have been publicly documented.

Storyly.history: Storyly was founded around 2020 as an in-app and web stories platform, initially operating from Istanbul, Turkey, before establishing US headquarters in New York. It is used by over 400 brands in 40+ countries including Sephora and Coca-Cola.
Storyly.summary_abuse: Storyly has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B SDK and platform that embeds story-format content into apps and websites for brands; it does not offer consumer-facing hosting where fraudsters could post phishing content.

StoryStream.history: StoryStream was founded around 2012 in Brighton, UK, as a user-generated content curation platform for brands. It later raised VC funding from MMC Ventures and evolved to include AI-powered visual commerce and shoppable UGC features.
StoryStream.summary_abuse: StoryStream has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B content curation and visual commerce platform used by established brands like L'Oreal and Lululemon, with no documented use in phishing or fraud contexts.

Straiv.history: Straiv was founded in 2015 in Stuttgart, Germany, by Alexander Haussmann, Fabian Hieber, and Patrick Luik under the name CODE2ORDER. The company focused on digitizing the hotel guest journey with check-in/check-out and messaging tools. In 2022, the company rebranded from CODE2ORDER to straiv, a portmanteau of 'strive' and Stuttgart. By 2025, straiv served over 3,400 hotels across 30 countries with a team of over 60 people.
Straiv.summary_abuse: Straiv is a B2B hotel software platform handling digital check-in and guest messaging for professional hospitality operators. Its use is restricted to contracted hotel partners, with no anonymous or consumer-facing sign-up pathway. No fraud, phishing, or abuse incidents involving Straiv infrastructure have been publicly documented.

Strands.history: Strands was founded in 2004 with operations in Oregon and Barcelona. The company initially developed personalization solutions for the music industry, some of which Apple acquired. The Strands Retail recommendation engine continues to serve ecommerce clients.
Strands.summary_abuse: Strands has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B personalization and recommendation engine for ecommerce platforms, operating behind merchant storefronts without consumer-facing hosting features that fraudsters would exploit.

Strapdown.js.history: Strapdown.js was created by arturadib around 2012 as a browser-side Markdown-to-HTML renderer using Bootstrap for styling. The original repository has had minimal activity; a maintained fork by Naereen continues the project.
Strapdown.js.summary_abuse: Strapdown.js has no abuse signal. It is a small client-side JavaScript library for rendering Markdown in the browser, with no network calls or consumer-facing surface that would attract fraudsters.

Strapi.history: Strapi was created in 2015 as a school project in Paris by Pierre Burgy, Aurélien Georget, and Jim Laurie, and became one of the most popular open-source headless CMSs. The company raised $10M Series A from Index Ventures in 2021 and $31M Series B in 2022.
Strapi.summary_abuse: Strapi has no notable abuse signal. It is a developer-focused open-source headless CMS used as a backend API layer; it runs on developer infrastructure and has no consumer-facing surface attractive to fraudsters.

Strato.history: Strato was founded in 1997 in Berlin, Germany as one of Europe's first web hosting providers. It was acquired by Deutsche Telekom and later by United Internet (1&1), becoming one of Germany's largest web hosting providers with millions of customers.
Strato.summary_abuse: Strato is a large European hosting provider, and like most shared hosting services it sees occasional abuse for phishing pages, spam, and fake storefronts. United Internet has abuse reporting processes and takedown procedures in place, though the scale of the platform means some abuse is expected.

Strato Website.history: Strato Website is the website builder product offered by Strato, a German hosting company owned by United Internet. It is a no-code drag-and-drop builder bundled with Strato's hosting plans, targeting small businesses in German-speaking markets.
Strato Website.summary_abuse: Strato Website has no notable standalone abuse signal. As a website builder tied to Strato hosting, abuse follows the same pattern as the hosting platform: low prevalence compared to standalone page builders, with standard anti-abuse procedures from the parent company.

Strattic.history: Strattic was founded in 2018 by Miriam Schwab and Josh Lawrence to provide static WordPress hosting. Elementor acquired Strattic in June 2022 to expand its WordPress ecosystem offerings.
Strattic.summary_abuse: Strattic has no notable abuse signal. It is a static WordPress hosting platform used by developers and agencies; by converting WordPress sites to static files, it actually reduces certain attack surfaces.

Strava.history: Strava was founded in 2009 in San Francisco by Mark Gainey and Michael Horvath as a social fitness tracking app for cyclists and runners. It has grown into one of the world's most popular fitness communities with over 100 million users worldwide.
Strava.summary_abuse: Strava has no notable abuse signal in the context of phishing or fraud. Privacy has been a concern, as Strava's heatmap feature in 2018 inadvertently revealed locations of secret military bases. The platform collects location data which raises general privacy considerations, but fraudulent use is not a documented issue.

Streamlit.history: Streamlit was created in 2018 by Adrien Treuille, Amanda Kelly, and Thiago Teixeira as an open-source Python framework for building data apps. Snowflake acquired Streamlit in March 2022 for approximately $800M to strengthen its data application capabilities.
Streamlit.summary_abuse: Streamlit has no notable abuse signal. It is a developer framework for building data applications, running on developer infrastructure. The Streamlit Community Cloud has been occasionally used to host demo apps with questionable content, but the platform enforces terms of service.

Streamlyne.history: Streamlyne is a small marketing automation SaaS for managing multi-channel digital campaigns; no public founding date or ownership changes have been recorded.
Streamlyne.summary_abuse: Streamlyne is a marketing automation platform with no notable abuse signal; it is a typical SMB marketing tool without characteristics that attract large-scale phishing or fraud activity.

Streamoid.history: Streamoid was founded in 2013 in India as an AI platform for fashion retail, initially providing styling intelligence and recommendation services. In 2023 it transitioned from services to a software product called CXO, an AI operating system for fashion brands.
Streamoid.summary_abuse: Streamoid is a B2B AI platform for fashion retailers with no consumer-facing surface. It has no notable abuse signal.

Streamroll.history: Streamroll is a niche SaaS CMS platform focused on apartment listings and affordable housing information management in the US; no founding date or ownership history is publicly documented.
Streamroll.summary_abuse: Streamroll is a specialized housing-listings CMS with no notable abuse signal; its narrow vertical use case does not make it a target for phishing or fraud campaigns.

Streamwood.history: Streamwood is a Russian-hosted live chat and callback SaaS (streamwood.ru); no founding date or ownership history is publicly available.
Streamwood.summary_abuse: Streamwood is a small live chat and callback widget with no notable abuse signal; it operates primarily in the Russian market without documented involvement in phishing or fraud campaigns.

Strife.history: Strife was founded around 2021 in Kalmar, Sweden, by team members from the former Swedish tech company Meridium, including co-founders Marcus Lindblom and Anders Granberg. It is a bootstrapped headless CMS startup with 2-10 employees.
Strife.summary_abuse: Strife is a small B2B headless CMS with no consumer-facing surface. It has no documented abuse signal.

Strikingly.history: Strikingly was founded in 2012 in Shanghai, China, by Teng Bao, Dafeng Guo, and David Haisha Chen. In 2013 it became the first Chinese startup to graduate from Y Combinator. It raised a Series A in 2017 and a Series A+ of $10 million in 2018 focused on China growth.
Strikingly.summary_abuse: Strikingly is a free website builder that has a documented history of being used to host phishing pages and fake storefronts, consistent with most free-tier website builders. Its free plan allows publishing sites on strikingly.com subdomains without payment, making it accessible for abuse with low friction. The vendor has an AUP and abuse reporting process, but enforcement responsiveness is not publicly benchmarked.

Stripe.history: Stripe was founded in 2010 by Patrick and John Collison in San Francisco as a payments API for developers. It grew into the leading global payment infrastructure company, raising over $2B in funding at a $95B valuation (2021) and processing hundreds of billions in payments annually.
Stripe.summary_abuse: Stripe has minimal abuse as a payment processor; legitimate use overwhelmingly dominates and Stripe employs sophisticated fraud detection including Radar. Fraudsters occasionally misuse Stripe's trial accounts to test stolen card details or set up fake businesses, but Stripe's KYC requirements, risk systems, and rapid account suspension make it one of the more abuse-resistant payment platforms.

Strolid.history: Strolid was founded in 2014 in Salem, New Hampshire, as an automotive BDC (Business Development Center) platform combining AI and human agents to manage dealer communications and CRM integrations. The company pioneered the vCon (virtual conversation) open data standard for AI-driven automotive conversations.
Strolid.summary_abuse: Strolid is a B2B automotive dealership communication platform. It handles customer calls and chat for car dealers, with no documented abuse signal relevant to phishing or fraud.

Struq.history: Struq was founded in London in 2008 by Sam Barnett as a personalized retargeting and dynamic creative optimization platform. It raised $8.5 million in 2012 and was acquired by Quantcast in October 2014, becoming part of Quantcast's personalized retargeting arm. The standalone Struq brand was effectively discontinued post-acquisition.
Struq.summary_abuse: Struq was an ad retargeting platform that tracked users across sites to deliver personalized ads, placing it in the ad-tech category with inherent privacy trade-offs. Like all retargeting platforms it collected behavioral data at scale. No specific fraud or malware abuse incidents are documented, but retargeting tech is privacy-invasive by design.

StrutFit.history: StrutFit was founded in New Zealand around 2018 to address the challenge of online footwear sizing using smartphone-based foot scanning and AI size recommendations. The company targets footwear retailers to reduce return rates caused by sizing issues.
StrutFit.summary_abuse: StrutFit is a B2B footwear sizing tool embedded on retailer websites. It processes foot scan images for size recommendations but has no documented abuse signal or misuse pattern.

STUDIO.history: STUDIO is a Japanese web design and hosting SaaS launched around 2017, developed to enable visual no-code website building with CMS and analytics included. It is popular in Japan and growing internationally as a Webflow alternative.
STUDIO.summary_abuse: STUDIO is a page builder SaaS that allows users to create and publish websites. It offers a free tier with hosting under studio.design subdomains and custom domain support on paid plans. Like all page builders with free hosting, it carries some risk of being used to create phishing or scam landing pages, though no large-scale documented abuse patterns are known.

Styla.history: Styla was founded in 2012 in Berlin, Germany, initially as a shoppable content platform. It evolved into a headless frontend and page builder for enterprise ecommerce, integrating with platforms like BigCommerce and Shopware. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes are on record.
Styla.summary_abuse: Styla is a B2B headless ecommerce frontend platform with no consumer-facing surface that would attract abuse. No documented abuse signal.

styled-components.history: styled-components was created by Glen Maddern and Max Stoiber in 2016 as a CSS-in-JS styling library for React, using tagged template literals. It became one of the most popular styling solutions in the React ecosystem.
styled-components.summary_abuse: styled-components has no notable abuse signal; it is a React styling library.

StyleSeat.history: StyleSeat was founded in 2011 in San Francisco as an appointment booking platform for beauty and wellness professionals. It has grown to over 400,000 professional profiles and handles salon and barber shop bookings.
StyleSeat.summary_abuse: StyleSeat has no notable abuse signal. It is a booking platform for hair and beauty professionals; while consumer-facing, it requires professional registration and has no anonymous hosting features that fraudsters typically exploit.

Stylitics.history: Stylitics was founded in 2011 in New York to provide automated outfitting and styling technology to fashion retailers. It serves major retail clients with AI-powered product recommendations and styling automation.
Stylitics.summary_abuse: Stylitics has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B styling automation platform serving enterprise fashion retailers with outfit recommendation tools; it has no consumer-facing hosting surface attractive to fraudsters.

Sub2Tech.history: Sub2Tech (Sub2 Technologies) is a customer data platform combining real-time consumer data with digital marketing technology. The company is based in the UK. No notable ownership changes are documented.
Sub2Tech.summary_abuse: Sub2Tech is a B2B customer data platform for digital advertising. It processes consumer data for targeting, which carries privacy risk, but is not documented as a tool used for phishing, fraud, or spam campaigns specifically.

Subbly.history: Subbly is a subscription ecommerce platform founded around 2016 in the UK, designed specifically for businesses operating on recurring subscription models.
Subbly.summary_abuse: Subbly has no notable abuse signal. It is a subscription ecommerce platform used by small businesses selling subscription boxes and recurring products, with no known use in fraud or phishing.

Subiz.history: Subiz is a Vietnamese live chat and customer messaging platform founded around 2012, consolidating multiple customer communication channels for businesses.
Subiz.summary_abuse: Subiz has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B live chat platform for businesses, without consumer-facing hosting features that would be attractive to fraudsters.

Sublime.history: Sublime was founded in 2012 in Paris as Sublime Skinz, a high-impact digital advertising platform focusing on skin formats and programmatic ad delivery. The company rebranded to Sublime around 2020 to reflect its broader product range. In September 2021 it was acquired by Azerion Group, a European gaming and digital advertising company.
Sublime.summary_abuse: Sublime is an ad-tech platform delivering high-impact display advertising. It collects behavioral data at scale for targeting purposes, placing it in the privacy-invasive ad-tech category. No specific fraud or malware hosting incidents are documented, but ad-tech platforms are inherently prone to malvertising risk if third-party ad chains are poorly audited.

SublimeVideo.history: SublimeVideo was an HTML5 video player framework launched around 2010 that provided cross-browser video playback. The project appears to have been abandoned as native browser video support matured.
SublimeVideo.summary_abuse: SublimeVideo has no abuse signal. It was a client-side HTML5 video player library with no consumer-facing network surface.

Submit Express.history: Submit Express was founded in 1998 by Pierre Zarokian in Burbank, California, as one of the earliest SEO agencies and platforms. The company survived multiple industry shifts and was recognized as a top SEO firm by Website Magazine in 2009.
Submit Express.summary_abuse: Submit Express is a legacy SEO services platform. The SEO tools category carries moderate abuse risk as such platforms can be misused for black-hat link building and spam SEO, though Submit Express itself has no documented major abuse incidents.

Subrion.history: Subrion is a free open-source PHP CMS developed by Intelliants. The project has been available on GitHub and SourceForge with releases spanning from around 2008. Maintenance activity has slowed in recent years, with no major releases documented in 2023-2024.
Subrion.summary_abuse: Subrion is a self-hosted CMS with no consumer-facing SaaS surface. Like all self-hosted CMS platforms, installations can be compromised and used for malware distribution if not patched, but there is no documented pattern of deliberate abuse specific to Subrion.

Subscribfy.history: Subscribfy was founded in 2023 in Sacramento, California, by operators who previously scaled DTC fashion brand Adore Me to $400 million in revenue before its acquisition by Victoria's Secret. The company built a Shopify-native subscription and membership management platform leveraging AI for DTC ecommerce retention.
Subscribfy.summary_abuse: Subscribfy is a Shopify subscription and membership management SaaS targeting legitimate DTC ecommerce brands. Its platform facilitates recurring billing for online stores and has no significant consumer-facing content hosting surface. No documented fraud or phishing incidents involving Subscribfy have been publicly reported.

Subscrimia.history: Subscrimia is a subscription management SaaS built specifically for BigCommerce stores, enabling recurring billing and subscription administration; no founding date or ownership history is publicly documented.
Subscrimia.summary_abuse: Subscrimia is a subscription billing tool for BigCommerce merchants with no notable abuse signal; its use is limited to legitimate recurring ecommerce scenarios.

Substack.history: Substack was founded in 2017 by Chris Best, Hamish McKenzie, and Jairaj Sethi in San Francisco as a newsletter and publishing platform. It raised $65M in 2021 and has grown to host thousands of paid newsletters across journalism, culture, and politics.
Substack.summary_abuse: Substack has moderate abuse risk as a publishing platform with free newsletter creation. It has been criticized for hosting misinformation, hate speech, and extremist content from some newsletters, and its policy of protecting creator revenue streams has made moderation decisions controversial. Spam newsletters and phishing emails have occasionally originated from Substack's infrastructure due to its legitimate email-sending reputation.

Subversion.history: Subversion (SVN) was created in 2000 by CollabNet as a successor to CVS, designed to fix CVS's known shortcomings. It became an Apache Software Foundation project in 2010 and was the dominant version control system before Git's rise, remaining widely used in enterprise environments.
Subversion.summary_abuse: Subversion has no abuse signal. It is a server-side version control system used by development teams; it has no consumer-facing surface and no characteristics that would make it attractive to fraudsters.

Sucuri.history: Sucuri was founded in 2010 as a website security company specializing in malware detection, cleanup, CDN, and WAF services. In March 2017, GoDaddy acquired Sucuri to advance its digital security offerings; Sucuri has continued to operate as a distinct brand under GoDaddy since the acquisition.
Sucuri.summary_abuse: Sucuri is a website security and CDN platform whose primary function is to defend against abuse rather than enable it. It provides WAF, DDoS mitigation, and malware cleanup services. No notable signal of the platform itself being weaponized for fraud or phishing.

Sugar.history: Sugar.js (also listed as Sugar) is a JavaScript utility library providing helper methods for working with native JavaScript objects like Date, String, and Array. It was created by Andrew Plummer and has been available since around 2010.
Sugar.summary_abuse: Sugar has no abuse signal. It is a client-side JavaScript utility library with no network calls or consumer-facing surface.

SugarJS.history: SugarJS is a JavaScript library that provides utility methods for native objects including dates, strings, and arrays. It was created by Andrew Plummer starting around 2010 and appears to be the same project as Sugar.js listed separately.
SugarJS.summary_abuse: SugarJS has no abuse signal. It is a client-side JavaScript utility library with no network calls or consumer-facing surface.

SugarWOD.history: SugarWOD was conceived in 2009 when founder Drew Larsen joined CrossFit and envisioned a workout tracking app for the community. The platform launched in 2013 as a free tool for CrossFit athletes and coaches, rapidly growing to serve over 2,200 gyms in 50+ countries representing 600,000 athletes. In 2019, SugarWOD was acquired by Daxko, owner of Zen Planner, to build a comprehensive affiliate fitness software suite. Founder Drew Larsen retired from the company in 2020.
SugarWOD.summary_abuse: SugarWOD is a fitness community and workout tracking app for CrossFit gyms, coaches, and athletes, with no general-purpose content hosting or financial transaction surface. Its community features are limited to workout tracking, leaderboards, and coach-athlete communication. No fraud, phishing, or spam incidents involving SugarWOD have been publicly documented.

Sugester.history: Sugester was founded on March 4, 2015 in Warsaw, Poland by Marcin Stefaniak, Michal Stefaniak, and Pawel Derylo. It originated as a side project from the creators of InvoiceOcean and received approximately $250,000 in funding from Inovo VC.
Sugester.summary_abuse: Sugester is a Polish CRM and helpdesk platform for SMBs. It has no documented abuse signal.

SuiteCRM.history: SuiteCRM was created in 2013 by SalesAgility in Edinburgh, UK, as an open-source fork of SugarCRM Community Edition when SugarCRM moved to a closed-source model. It has grown into the most widely deployed open-source CRM alternative.
SuiteCRM.summary_abuse: SuiteCRM has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted open-source CRM used by businesses to manage customer relationships, with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

Suiteshare.history: Suiteshare was founded in 2018 in Porto Alegre, Brazil, as a platform enabling retail transactions via WhatsApp. It connected 10 million buyers to over 400 brands before being acquired by VTEX in May 2021 to strengthen VTEX's conversational commerce offering.
Suiteshare.summary_abuse: Suiteshare is a conversational commerce platform enabling WhatsApp-based retail, now owned by VTEX. No documented abuse signal, though WhatsApp commerce tools can be misused for spam outreach if misconfigured.

Sulu.history: Sulu is an open-source PHP CMS built on the Symfony framework, developed by the Austrian company massiveart. It was first released around 2013 and targets enterprise and agency use cases.
Sulu.summary_abuse: Sulu has no abuse signal. It is a self-hosted PHP CMS used by developers and agencies for enterprise web projects, with no consumer-facing hosting surface that fraudsters would exploit.

SummerCart.history: SummerCart is a self-hosted PHP ecommerce platform by Turnkey Web Tools, Inc., a San Diego-based company established in 2001. The platform targets small to medium-sized businesses and has been a stable niche offering for over two decades.
SummerCart.summary_abuse: SummerCart is a self-hosted PHP ecommerce platform. Like other self-hosted ecommerce software, it can be deployed on compromised or bulletproof hosting by bad actors, but the platform itself has no documented abuse-specific incidents.

Summernote.history: Summernote is an open-source JavaScript WYSIWYG rich text editor first released in 2013. It is built on Bootstrap and jQuery, and has been widely adopted in web applications for inline editing.
Summernote.summary_abuse: Summernote has minimal abuse signal as an open-source rich text editor library. It is a client-side component that runs in web applications; XSS vulnerabilities in Summernote integrations have been reported historically when user content is not properly sanitized, but the library itself is not an abuse vector.

Sumo.history: Sumo (formerly SumoMe) is a suite of website marketing tools founded around 2013 by AppSumo's Noah Kagan. It provides email list building, pop-ups, heat maps, and social sharing tools for websites.
Sumo.summary_abuse: Sumo is a marketing tool plugin that injects pop-ups and email capture forms into websites. Its heavy use of third-party trackers and aggressive pop-up patterns are more of a dark pattern concern than fraud abuse. No documented use in phishing or malware distribution.

SumUp.history: SumUp was founded in 2012 in Berlin by Marc-Alexander Christ, Stefan Jeschonnek, and Jan Deepen as a mobile card payment solution targeting small merchants. It raised $590M in 2022, reached unicorn status, and serves millions of merchants across 30+ countries.
SumUp.summary_abuse: SumUp has minimal abuse risk. As a regulated payment processor it requires business verification and card reader hardware, making it significantly harder to abuse than purely digital payment channels. Fraudulent merchants are occasionally caught but SumUp enforces KYC requirements and PCI-DSS compliance.

Suncel.history: Suncel is a headless CMS built specifically for Next.js, likely launched around 2022. It targets developers and marketers needing a visual builder integrated with the Next.js ecosystem. No notable ownership changes on record.
Suncel.summary_abuse: Suncel is a developer-focused headless CMS for Next.js with no documented abuse signal.

SunOS.history: SunOS was developed by Sun Microsystems starting in 1982 as a Unix-based operating system for their workstations and servers. Sun was acquired by Oracle in 2010, and SunOS as a distinct product has been superseded by Oracle Solaris. Legacy SunOS 4.x systems still exist in some long-running environments.
SunOS.summary_abuse: SunOS has no abuse signal. It is a Unix operating system running on server infrastructure with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

Sunshop.history: Sunshop is an ecommerce software platform developed by Turnkey Web Tools, Inc., a San Diego company established in 2001. It offers both hosted and self-hosted options for small and medium businesses. The company also offers white-label versions of the platform.
Sunshop.summary_abuse: Sunshop is a long-running SMB ecommerce platform with no documented abuse-specific incidents. It supports white-label reselling which marginally increases risk of unvetted storefronts, but no fraud patterns are documented.

Supabase.history: Supabase was founded in 2020 by Paul Copplestone and Ant Wilson as an open-source alternative to Firebase, built on PostgreSQL. It raised $80M Series B in 2022 and has grown rapidly as a popular backend-as-a-service for developers.
Supabase.summary_abuse: Supabase has minimal abuse signal. Its free tier with database and auth hosting has been used in some hobby and demo projects that may violate terms, but the platform actively enforces usage limits and requires signup. No documented use in phishing or fraud infrastructure.

Supademo.history: Supademo is an interactive product demo platform founded around 2022, targeting product and marketing teams in SaaS companies.
Supademo.summary_abuse: Supademo has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B product demo tool with no consumer-facing hosting features that fraudsters would exploit.

Super Builder.history: Super (super.so) launched in 2020 as a tool for converting Notion pages into custom websites. It won the Product Hunt 2020 Golden Kitty Award in its category. The platform requires a Notion workspace and serves as a thin publishing layer on top of Notion content, charging per-site monthly fees starting at $16.
Super Builder.summary_abuse: Super.so is a Notion-to-website publishing service. Because it converts existing Notion pages into public websites, it could theoretically be used to rapidly publish phishing landing pages or fake content sites, though this would require a Notion account. No documented large-scale phishing campaigns using Super.so infrastructure have been publicly reported, but the ease of publishing arbitrary content warrants moderate caution.

Super Socializer.history: Super Socializer is a WordPress plugin for social login and sharing functionality, developed by Team Heateor.
Super Socializer.summary_abuse: Super Socializer has no notable abuse signal. It is a WordPress social login plugin with no consumer-facing hosting surface that fraudsters would exploit.

Superblog.history: Superblog was founded in 2020 in Bengaluru, India by solo founder Sai Krishna as an SEO-optimized, fast-loading blogging platform for startups and founders. By 2023 it had reached approximately $81K ARR as a bootstrapped solo project.
Superblog.summary_abuse: Superblog is a blogging-as-a-service platform with no documented abuse signal. Custom domain support is available, which is a standard feature with low abuse risk for this category.

SuperBuzz.history: SuperBuzz was incorporated in Israel on January 10, 2018, as an AI-powered push notification marketing platform. The company completed a qualifying transaction and became publicly traded on the TSX Venture Exchange under the symbol SPZ.
SuperBuzz.summary_abuse: SuperBuzz is a push notification marketing platform. Push notification platforms carry moderate abuse risk as they can be misused for spam push campaigns or to track users across sites without adequate consent. No specific documented abuse incidents for SuperBuzz.

Superchat.history: Superchat was founded in July 2020 in Berlin by Mika Hally and Yilmaz Koknar. The company raised a seed round of €2.8 million from 468 Capital, then a Series A of €13.6 million led by Blossom Capital in January 2022, reaching total funding of approximately $19 million.
Superchat.summary_abuse: Superchat is a B2B omnichannel messaging inbox platform for SMBs, primarily serving German-speaking markets. It aggregates WhatsApp, email, SMS, and social messaging. No documented abuse signal.

Superform.history: Superform is a Webflow-native form engine designed for no-code users to build and manage forms without coding; no founding date or notable ownership history is publicly available.
Superform.summary_abuse: Superform is a Webflow form tool with no notable abuse signal; as a form-builder it has no consumer-facing surface that specifically attracts phishing or fraud.

SuperHote.history: SuperHote was founded in 2016 in France by Mathieu and Gaetan, who were themselves Airbnb hosts, to build a channel manager and vacation rental management platform for short-term rental operators.
SuperHote.summary_abuse: SuperHote is a vacation rental channel manager primarily serving the French market. It has no documented abuse signal.

SuperLemon app.history: SuperLemon app is an all-in-one WhatsApp plugin for Shopify stores enabling chat, automated messaging, and marketing features. It was developed by a small startup, likely based in India, serving Shopify merchants globally.
SuperLemon app.summary_abuse: SuperLemon is a WhatsApp chat and marketing plugin for Shopify. WhatsApp-based messaging tools can be misused for unsolicited marketing, but no specific documented abuse incidents are on record for this app.

SuperPluralAnalytics.history: SuperPluralAnalytics is a web analytics tool offered by Superplural; no notable founding date or ownership history is publicly available.
SuperPluralAnalytics.summary_abuse: SuperPluralAnalytics is a web analytics service with no notable abuse signal; as an analytics product it has no consumer-facing surface used for phishing or fraud.

SuperPWA.history: SuperPWA was launched around 2017 as a WordPress plugin to convert WordPress sites into Progressive Web Apps without coding; it has been widely adopted on WordPress.org and released a Pro version for additional features.
SuperPWA.summary_abuse: SuperPWA is a WordPress PWA plugin with no notable abuse signal; it is a site-enhancement tool with no features that attract phishing or fraud activity.

Supersized.history: Supersized was a jQuery plugin for creating fullscreen background slideshows, created around 2009. It has been abandoned as CSS and modern JavaScript approaches replaced the need for such libraries.
Supersized.summary_abuse: Supersized has no abuse signal. It was a client-side JavaScript background slideshow library with no network calls or consumer-facing surface.

Superspeed.history: Superspeed is a Shopify app that optimises page speed for Shopify-hosted storefronts; no founding date or notable history is publicly available.
Superspeed.summary_abuse: Superspeed is a Shopify performance optimization app with no notable abuse signal; it operates exclusively on Shopify stores and has no consumer-facing surface used for fraud.

SuperStats.history: SuperStats is an early web analytics service that has offered website traffic statistics since around the early 2000s; no notable ownership changes are documented.
SuperStats.summary_abuse: SuperStats is a web traffic analytics tool with no notable abuse signal; it is a passive measurement service with no consumer-facing surface attracting fraud.

Support Hero.history: Support Hero was founded in 2016 and is based in Cincinnati, Ohio. It was built as a lightweight knowledge base widget that can be embedded in websites to deflect inbound support tickets. The company operates with a small team of 2-10 people and has remained independently operated.
Support Hero.summary_abuse: Support Hero is a lightweight B2B knowledge base widget with no consumer-facing hosting or user-generated content surface. Its function is to embed searchable help docs in business websites. No abuse, phishing, or fraud incidents involving Support Hero have been documented.

Supsystic.history: Supsystic is a WordPress plugin developer offering opt-in popup, data table, and pricing table plugins. The company appears to be Ukraine-based with a suite of freemium WordPress products.
Supsystic.summary_abuse: Supsystic provides WordPress popup plugins for email list building. Popup tools can be used to create misleading opt-in forms, but no specific abuse incidents are documented for this vendor.

Surfside.history: Surfside was launched around 2019 by Jon Lowen and Michael Blanche, veterans of SITO Mobile, as a first-party data and commerce media platform initially targeting the cannabis industry. The company raised $4 million in seed funding in 2021 from investors including Casa Verde Capital.
Surfside.summary_abuse: Surfside is an ad-tech and data activation platform focused on the cannabis industry. It collects and aggregates first-party data from POS systems and loyalty programs for ad targeting, placing it in the privacy-sensitive ad-tech category. No specific fraud or malware abuse incidents are documented.

Surge.history: Surge is a static web publishing platform launched around 2014, designed for front-end developers to publish static sites directly from the command line via npm.
Surge.summary_abuse: Surge has some abuse risk as a free static hosting platform with custom domain support. Its free tier with easy CLI deployment and custom domain support has been occasionally used to host phishing pages and malicious static sites. The platform is small and developer-focused which limits but doesn't eliminate abuse.

Survale.history: Survale is an employer satisfaction and recruitment experience platform founded around 2015, targeting HR teams and talent acquisition professionals. No notable ownership changes or major events are documented.
Survale.summary_abuse: Survale is an HR and recruitment satisfaction platform with no notable abuse signal. It is a niche B2B SaaS targeting enterprise HR teams, with no consumer-facing surface that would attract fraud or phishing abuse.

Survicate.history: Survicate was founded in 2013 in Warsaw, Poland, by Kamil Rejent. The company raised approximately $1 million from Airbridge Equity Partners in 2017 to fund international expansion. The platform grew to $4.4 million in annual revenue by 2025, serving 1,600 customers with email, NPS, website, and in-app survey tools.
Survicate.summary_abuse: Survicate is a customer feedback and survey SaaS used by businesses to collect product and service feedback. As an embedded survey tool, it has minimal direct abuse surface. Some risk exists in that survey links could be used to collect data deceptively, but this is a generic risk for survey tools rather than a documented Survicate-specific issue. No notable abuse incidents involving Survicate have been publicly reported.

SUSE.history: SUSE was founded in Germany in 1992 as one of the first commercial Linux distributions. It was acquired by Novell in 2003, then by The Attachmate Group in 2011, which was itself acquired by Micro Focus in 2014. In 2019, EQT Partners spun off SUSE as an independent company, and SUSE went public on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange in 2021. SUSE is known for SUSE Linux Enterprise (SLE) and the community openSUSE distribution.
SUSE.summary_abuse: SUSE is a Linux server operating system with no notable abuse signal. It is enterprise infrastructure software used by large organizations and has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

Svbtle.history: Svbtle was created by Dustin Curtis and launched in 2012 as an invite-only minimalist blogging platform, later opened to the public. It emphasizes clean writing over features and is maintained as a small independent product.
Svbtle.summary_abuse: Svbtle is a minimalist blogging platform with low abuse risk. Users host content under subdomains of svbtle.com and the platform has a small user base. No significant documented abuse pattern is associated with it.

Svelte.history: Svelte was created by Rich Harris and first released in 2016. Unlike virtual-DOM-based frameworks, it compiles components to efficient vanilla JavaScript at build time. Rich Harris joined Vercel in 2021, which now sponsors Svelte development, though the project remains independently governed by the Svelte core team under an MIT license.
Svelte.summary_abuse: Svelte is a front-end compiler and framework with no notable abuse signal. It is a developer tool that outputs compiled JavaScript and has no consumer-facing surface that fraud actors would target.

SvelteKit.history: SvelteKit is the official full-stack application framework for Svelte, released in beta in 2020 and reaching 1.0 in December 2022. It was developed by the Svelte core team as the successor to Sapper, offering a filesystem-based router, SSR, and seamless deployment adapters. Vercel sponsors development through Rich Harris.
SvelteKit.summary_abuse: SvelteKit is a web application framework with no notable abuse signal. It is a developer tool used to build web applications and has no direct consumer-facing surface that fraud actors would target.

Sverve.history: Sverve was a content curation and influencer marketing platform connecting brands with bloggers and social media users. It appears to have been active around 2013-2015 and has since become largely dormant.
Sverve.summary_abuse: Sverve is a niche influencer marketing and content curation platform with no specific documented abuse signal. Its apparent dormancy and small user base limit both its utility and its abuse surface.

SVG Support.history: SVG Support is a free open-source WordPress plugin that enables safe SVG file uploads to the media library. It is maintained by a single developer and available on WordPress.org.
SVG Support.summary_abuse: SVG Support is a WordPress plugin for enabling SVG uploads with no notable direct abuse signal. Poorly configured SVG upload capabilities can be a vector for stored XSS attacks, but the plugin includes sanitization features to mitigate this risk.

Swagger UI.history: Swagger UI was created by Tony Tam at Wordnik in 2011 as part of the Swagger specification for REST API documentation. SmartBear Software acquired the Swagger tools in 2015 and donated the specification to the OpenAPI Initiative under the Linux Foundation, renaming it OpenAPI. Swagger UI itself remains Apache-licensed and is maintained by SmartBear under the swagger-api GitHub organization.
Swagger UI.summary_abuse: Swagger UI is an API documentation tool with no notable abuse signal. It is developer-facing infrastructure used to render API docs and has no consumer-facing surface that would attract fraud or phishing abuse.

Swagify.history: Swagify is a small e-commerce upsell and offer customization SaaS targeting online stores. It offers cross-sell and promotional offer creation tools. Limited public history is available.
Swagify.summary_abuse: Swagify is a niche e-commerce upsell tool with no documented abuse signal. It is a small B2B SaaS for online merchants and does not offer consumer-facing hosting or anonymous access features.

Swapcard.history: Swapcard was founded in 2013 in Paris, France, as an event networking and engagement platform. It grew to serve large conferences and trade shows with AI-powered matchmaking, attendee networking, and monetization tools. The company has raised venture funding and expanded internationally.
Swapcard.summary_abuse: Swapcard is an event networking and engagement platform with no documented abuse signal. It is primarily used by event organizers and conference attendees and does not expose anonymous content-hosting features that would attract fraud.

Swat.io.history: Swat.io was founded around 2012 in Vienna, Austria, by Michael Kamleitner and team. The platform grew to serve over 30,000 users across retail, tourism, and public administration sectors in the DACH region and beyond. Kamleitner also co-founded Walls.io, a social media aggregation tool that shares company roots with Swat.io.
Swat.io.summary_abuse: Swat.io is a B2B social media management platform used by teams to schedule and manage social posts. It operates under EU GDPR rules as an Austrian company. No documented phishing, fraud, or platform abuse incidents involving Swat.io have been publicly reported.

Swaven.history: Swaven is a Where-To-Buy technology platform founded in France that helps consumer brands connect online shoppers to retail purchasing options. It serves FMCG and consumer goods brands by embedding purchase-path widgets on brand websites.
Swaven.summary_abuse: Swaven is a brand-to-retail purchase-path SaaS with no documented abuse signal. It is a B2B tool for consumer goods brands and does not expose consumer-facing hosting or anonymous access features.

SWC.history: SWC (Speedy Web Compiler) is an open-source Rust-based JavaScript and TypeScript compiler created by Donnybrook Park in 2019. It was designed as a significantly faster alternative to Babel for transpiling modern JavaScript. Next.js adopted SWC as its default compiler in version 12 (2021), dramatically accelerating its adoption across the JavaScript ecosystem.
SWC.summary_abuse: SWC is a developer build-tool compiler with no consumer-facing surface and no notable abuse signal.

Sweet Analytics.history: Sweet Analytics is a UK-based e-commerce analytics platform that consolidates sales and marketing data from multiple sources for online retailers. It targets direct-to-consumer brands seeking unified reporting.
Sweet Analytics.summary_abuse: Sweet Analytics is a niche e-commerce analytics SaaS with no documented abuse signal. It is a B2B data consolidation tool for online retailers with no consumer-facing hosting features.

Sweet Upsell.history: Sweet Upsell is a small Shopify app that automates product upsell and cross-sell offers for e-commerce stores. It is a niche tool in the Shopify app ecosystem with limited public history.
Sweet Upsell.summary_abuse: Sweet Upsell is a Shopify upsell automation app with no documented abuse signal. It is a small B2B tool for Shopify merchants and does not expose consumer-facing hosting features.

SweetAlert.history: SweetAlert was created by Tristan Edwards and released in 2014 as a JavaScript library offering stylized browser alert replacements. It gained wide popularity but development has slowed over the years. SweetAlert2 was created as an independent successor with more features.
SweetAlert.summary_abuse: SweetAlert is a JavaScript UI library with no notable abuse signal. It is a front-end developer tool for replacing browser dialogs and has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

SweetAlert2.history: SweetAlert2 was created by Limon Monte in 2016 as an actively maintained and extended successor to SweetAlert, offering more customization, accessibility, and responsive design. It is MIT-licensed and maintained by an independent open-source community.
SweetAlert2.summary_abuse: SweetAlert2 is a JavaScript modal dialog library with no notable abuse signal. It is a front-end developer tool with no consumer-facing surface that fraud actors would target.

SweetHelp.history: SweetHelp is a small SaaS that sends WhatsApp and SMS messages to recover abandoned shopping carts for e-commerce stores. It is a niche tool with limited public history.
SweetHelp.summary_abuse: SweetHelp sends SMS and WhatsApp messages for abandoned cart recovery. Tools that send mass SMS and WhatsApp messages carry inherent risk of being used for spam if improperly configured, though its primary market is legitimate e-commerce merchants. No specific documented abuse incidents are known.

Swell.history: Swell is a headless e-commerce platform founded around 2019, offering an API-first approach for modern brands and agencies to build custom storefronts. It targets developers building custom e-commerce experiences outside traditional platforms like Shopify.
Swell.summary_abuse: Swell is a headless e-commerce platform with no documented abuse signal. It targets developers and brands building custom storefronts and does not have a consumer-facing free hosting tier commonly exploited for fraud.

Swell CX.history: Swell CX is a small SaaS offering online review collection and website visitor chat tools for businesses. It targets small businesses wanting to build their online reputation through customer reviews.
Swell CX.summary_abuse: Swell CX is a review management and chat SaaS with no documented abuse signal. It is a small B2B tool for local businesses and online reputation management with no notable fraud exposure.

Swetrix.history: Swetrix is an open-source, privacy-focused web analytics platform launched around 2021, positioning itself as a GDPR-compliant alternative to Google Analytics. It is developed by a small independent team and offers both a hosted SaaS and self-hosted deployment.
Swetrix.summary_abuse: Swetrix is an open-source analytics tool with no notable abuse signal. It is a privacy-focused backend analytics platform with no consumer-facing hosting features that would attract fraud.

SWFObject.history: SWFObject is an open-source JavaScript library created around 2006 to reliably embed Adobe Flash SWF files in web pages, solving cross-browser inconsistencies. It was widely used during the Flash era. With Adobe Flash officially deprecated and end-of-lifed on December 31, 2020, SWFObject has no practical use and is now itself deprecated.
SWFObject.summary_abuse: SWFObject is an abandoned JavaScript library for embedding Flash content. Flash itself is deprecated and blocked by all modern browsers, making SWFObject effectively obsolete with no active abuse surface.

Swiffy Slider.history: Swiffy Slider is a lightweight open-source HTML/CSS/JS slider and carousel component library. It is an independently maintained project with no notable ownership changes.
Swiffy Slider.summary_abuse: Swiffy Slider is a frontend slider component library with no abuse signal. It is a developer tool for building web page carousels and has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

Swifty.history: Swifty is a marketing automation SaaS targeting property management and leasing companies, focused on converting rental leads into lease signings. It is a niche vertical SaaS with limited public history.
Swifty.summary_abuse: Swifty is a niche marketing automation tool for property management companies with no documented abuse signal. Its B2B vertical focus and lack of consumer-facing hosting features mean minimal fraud exposure.

Swiftype.history: Swiftype was founded in 2012 as a site search service for websites and apps. It was acquired by Elastic (the company behind Elasticsearch and the Elastic Stack) in 2017. After the acquisition, Elastic has largely redirected customers toward its core Elasticsearch products.
Swiftype.summary_abuse: Swiftype is a site search SaaS acquired by Elastic with no documented abuse signal. It is a backend developer service for indexing and serving search results with no consumer-facing hosting surface.

Swipe Pages.history: Swipe Pages is a landing page builder founded around 2019, targeting performance marketers and agencies seeking AMP-enabled mobile landing pages. It is based in India and has grown in the marketing automation space.
Swipe Pages.summary_abuse: Swipe Pages is a landing page builder that allows users to create and host pages under custom domains. Like most landing page platforms, it carries moderate abuse risk since it can be used to host phishing or scam landing pages. The vendor has an AUP and standard takedown mechanisms typical of this category.

Swiper.history: Swiper is a widely-used open-source JavaScript slider/carousel library created by Vladimir Kharlampidi (nolimits4web). First released around 2014, it has become the most popular modern touch slider library, with tens of millions of npm downloads monthly. It is MIT-licensed and maintained by its original creator.
Swiper.summary_abuse: Swiper is a JavaScript carousel/slider library with no notable abuse signal. It is a front-end developer tool for building touch-enabled slideshows and carousels with no consumer-facing surface that fraud actors would target.

Swoogo.history: Swoogo was founded in 2015 by Leonora Valvo, Tim Cummins, and Neil Keefe as an event management software platform. The company raised $20 million across two funding rounds. In May 2020, Chris Sykes acquired Swoogo and became CEO, relocating the headquarters to Los Angeles, California.
Swoogo.summary_abuse: Swoogo is a professional event management and registration SaaS used by corporate event organizers. Its B2B registration platform has limited consumer-facing content hosting exposure. No documented fraud or phishing incidents involving Swoogo infrastructure have been publicly reported.

Swoop.history: Swoop was founded in 2011 by Ron Elwell and Simeon Simeonov as Shopximity, later pivoting to healthcare advertising and rebranding as Swoop. The company focuses on privacy-safe pharmaceutical audience targeting and AI-driven patient and HCP marketing, serving over 30 of the world's largest pharmaceutical manufacturers.
Swoop.summary_abuse: Swoop is an ad-tech platform focused on pharmaceutical marketing and audience targeting. As an advertising technology company, it collects and processes audience data for targeting purposes, which carries inherent privacy trade-offs. There is no documented pattern of fraudulent abuse, but as a data-driven advertising platform it participates in the broader ad-tech ecosystem, which can sometimes be exploited for mis-targeting or data misuse.

Swup.history: Swup is an open-source JavaScript library for page transitions on server-rendered websites, created around 2018. It is community-maintained on GitHub under the MIT license and has no commercial backer.
Swup.summary_abuse: Swup has no notable abuse signal. It is a frontend JavaScript library for page transitions with no consumer-facing surface and no hosting or data-collection features that would attract fraudsters.

Swym Wishlist Plus.history: Swym Corporation was founded in 2012 and joined the Techstars Seattle 2017 accelerator cohort. The company focuses on shopper engagement tools for ecommerce, with Wishlist Plus being its flagship Shopify app, now used by over 50,000 merchants.
Swym Wishlist Plus.summary_abuse: Swym Wishlist Plus has no notable abuse signal. It is a merchant-side Shopify app for wishlist functionality with no direct consumer-hosting surface that would attract fraudsters.

Sylius.history: Sylius was created by Pawel Janas in Poland around 2011 as an open-source ecommerce framework built on Symfony. It is maintained by Sylius Ltd and has grown a commercial support and cloud offering around the MIT-licensed core. No major ownership changes or deprecation events have occurred.
Sylius.summary_abuse: Sylius has no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted PHP ecommerce framework that site owners deploy on their own infrastructure; there is no hosted consumer surface that scammers can exploit directly.

Symbolset.history: Symbolset is a font-based icon system that uses ligatures to convert words into icons, created around 2012. It appears to be a small independent project with limited ongoing development.
Symbolset.summary_abuse: Symbolset has no notable abuse signal. It is a font icon tool delivered as a web font resource with no consumer-hosting or data-collection features that would attract fraudsters.

Symfony.history: Symfony was created by Fabien Potencier at SensioLabs in France and first released in 2005. It became one of the most influential PHP frameworks, with many of its components adopted by other projects including Laravel and Drupal. SensioLabs provides commercial support and training; the core framework is MIT-licensed.
Symfony.summary_abuse: Symfony has no notable abuse signal. It is a backend PHP framework that runs on developer-controlled servers; there is no hosted consumer surface that scammers can exploit.

Sympa.history: Sympa (Systeme de Multi-Postage Automatique) is an open-source mailing list manager originally developed at RENATER in France, with roots dating to the late 1990s. It is widely used by academic and research institutions in Europe. No major ownership changes; it remains community-maintained under the GPL license.
Sympa.summary_abuse: Sympa is a self-hosted mailing list server primarily used by universities and research networks. It could theoretically be misconfigured to relay spam, but this is an operational risk shared by all open mailing list software and not a documented abuse pattern specific to Sympa.

Synamate.history: Synamate is an AI-powered marketing automation and lead management SaaS; no founding date or ownership history is publicly documented.
Synamate.summary_abuse: Synamate is a marketing automation platform with no notable abuse signal; it is a typical AI-powered SMB marketing tool with no characteristics particularly attractive to fraudsters.

SynBird.history: SynBird is a SaaS appointment scheduling and queue management platform for community and government services; no founding date or ownership history is publicly available.
SynBird.summary_abuse: SynBird is an appointment scheduling tool for public and community services with no notable abuse signal; its use case does not attract phishing or fraud activity.

Syncee.history: Syncee was founded in 2015 in Debrecen, Hungary, by Akos Gulyas. The platform grew to connect over 200,000 online merchants with 12,000+ vetted suppliers for dropshipping and wholesale. It is available on Shopify, BigCommerce, and other ecommerce platforms.
Syncee.summary_abuse: Syncee is a B2B dropshipping and wholesale marketplace connecting legitimate ecommerce merchants with suppliers. As a product catalog and order automation tool, it does not directly enable fake storefronts, but merchants using the platform could theoretically resell counterfeit goods. The platform vets suppliers and targets established online retailers rather than anonymous users. No documented fraud incidents specifically linked to Syncee have been publicly reported.

Syncfusion.history: Syncfusion was founded in 2001 in Morrisville, North Carolina, providing UI component libraries for .NET and web development. It became well-known for its community license program offering free use to individual developers and small businesses. No major ownership changes have occurred.
Syncfusion.summary_abuse: Syncfusion has no notable abuse signal. It is a UI component library for desktop and web applications with no consumer-facing hosted surface that scammers would target.

Syncle.history: Syncle is a niche SaaS that automatically embeds YouTube product video reviews into online shops; no founding date or ownership history is publicly documented.
Syncle.summary_abuse: Syncle is a video review integration tool for ecommerce stores with no notable abuse signal; it has no consumer-facing hosting features that attract phishing or fraud.

Synctrack.history: Synctrack is a Shopify order tracking and returns management app developed by a small team, likely Vietnam-based. No notable ownership changes or public incidents documented.
Synctrack.summary_abuse: Synctrack has no notable abuse signal. It is an order tracking tool for Shopify merchants with no direct consumer-hosting surface exploitable for fraud.

Syndeca.history: Syndeca is a visual commerce platform for brands to create shoppable digital campaigns. It appears to be a small independent company with limited public history.
Syndeca.summary_abuse: Syndeca has no notable abuse signal. It is a brand-facing visual commerce platform with no documented exploitation for fraud.

Synerise.history: Synerise was founded in 2013 in Poland by Jaroslaw Krolewski, Krzysztof Kochmanski, and Milosz Balus as an AI-driven behavioral data and customer data platform. It raised a €21.9 million funding round and closed a $23 million Series B in 2022 to fuel international expansion.
Synerise.summary_abuse: Synerise is a B2B analytics and customer data platform with no notable abuse signal. It processes behavioral data for marketing personalization and does not expose file hosting, anonymous sign-up, or mass-messaging features that attract fraudsters.

Synology DiskStation.history: Synology was founded in 2000 in Taiwan and is best known for its NAS (network-attached storage) hardware running the DiskStation Manager (DSM) operating system. It has grown into a major brand for home and business storage solutions worldwide, with no major ownership changes.
Synology DiskStation.summary_abuse: Synology DiskStation has a limited abuse signal. Exposed or misconfigured Synology NAS devices have been targeted by ransomware campaigns and brute-force attacks historically (e.g. the SynoLocker ransomware in 2014), but this reflects end-user misconfiguration rather than inherent vendor abuse tolerance. Synology maintains an active security advisory program and a bug bounty.

SyntaxHighlighter.history: SyntaxHighlighter is a JavaScript syntax highlighting library created by Alex Gorbatchev around 2004, widely used in WordPress blogs and CMS platforms for displaying formatted code snippets. The project has seen limited updates in recent years.
SyntaxHighlighter.summary_abuse: SyntaxHighlighter has no notable abuse signal. It is a client-side JavaScript code highlighting library with no hosting or data-collection surface.

Synup.history: Synup was founded in 2013-2014 in New York City by Ashwin Ramesh, Karthik Krishnamoorthy, and Mohan Gopalakrishnan. It launched services in June 2014 and received seed funding from Angel Prime. The company raised $6.5 million total across two rounds through 2017 and grew to $10.4 million in annual revenue by 2024 serving 167,000 customers.
Synup.summary_abuse: Synup is a local listings and reputation management SaaS used by businesses to manage their presence on directories and review platforms. Its toolset manages business data rather than hosting user-generated content for consumers. No documented phishing, fraud, or abuse incidents specifically involving Synup infrastructure have been publicly reported.

Systema.history: Systema is an anonymous personalization SaaS for retail, delivering tailored experiences without personal identifiers; no founding date or ownership history is publicly documented.
Systema.summary_abuse: Systema is a privacy-preserving retail personalization tool with no notable abuse signal; its anonymous-by-design approach reduces risk of personal data misuse.

Systeme.io.history: Systeme.io was founded in 2018 by Aurelien Amacker in France as an all-in-one online business platform. It gained traction by offering a generous free tier competing with ClickFunnels and Kajabi. No major ownership changes documented.
Systeme.io.summary_abuse: Systeme.io is an all-in-one marketing platform including funnel builders and email marketing. Like other mass-email and funnel-building tools, it could be misused for spam or phishing funnels, but no specific large-scale documented abuse incidents are known. The platform offers a free tier which lowers the barrier for misuse.

Systems Accel.history: Systems Accel is an all-in-one sales and marketing automation platform for coaches, consultants, and service providers; no founding date or ownership history is publicly documented.
Systems Accel.summary_abuse: Systems Accel is a marketing automation platform targeted at small service businesses with no notable abuse signal; it is a typical SMB automation tool without documented fraud or phishing involvement.

Syte.history: Syte was founded around 2015 in Israel and provides visual AI for retail product discovery. The company has raised VC funding and serves enterprise fashion and home goods retailers.
Syte.summary_abuse: Syte has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise visual AI product discovery tool with no consumer-facing hosted surface exploitable for fraud.
